Data mining is the process of extracting insights from large datasets using statistical and computational techniques.
It can involve structured, semi-structured, or unstructured data stored in databases, data warehouses, or data lakes.
The goal is to uncover hidden patterns and relationships to support informed decision-making and predictions.
Early statistical foundations were laid by Bayes, Gauss, Laplace, and Galton.
Probability theory and regression analysis emerged along with early data tabulation devices such as Hollerith’s punch card tabulator.
The rise of database management systems and relational models occurred during the 1950s–1970s.
This slide provides an overview of the topics we will cover today.
The term “data mining” and “knowledge discovery in databases (KDD)” solidified in the late 1980s.
Scalable algorithms for association, clustering, and classification were introduced along with the first KDD workshop in 1989.
From the 2000s to the present, data mining integrated with big data, cloud computing, deep learning, and AI.
Predictive maintenance in manufacturing uses sensor data to prevent equipment failures.
Fraud detection identifies suspicious behavior in financial transactions.
Customer segmentation improves marketing and recommendations in e-commerce.
Network intrusion detection identifies abnormalities in network traffic.
Process optimization improves robotics and smart sensor networks.
Resource allocation and supply chain management use prediction and workflow mining.
The KDD process includes understanding the domain, data preparation, modeling, evaluation, and acting on discovered knowledge.
The CRISP-DM framework includes business understanding, data understanding, preparation, modeling, evaluation, and deployment.
Descriptive data mining includes clustering, association rule mining, and summarization.
Predictive data mining includes classification, regression, anomaly detection, and forecasting.
Data mining faces quality issues due to inaccurate, missing, or inconsistent data.
Data complexity arises from unstructured, multimedia, time series, and streaming data.
Data privacy concerns involve anonymization and compliance with regulations like GDPR and HIPAA.
Scalability challenges involve handling high-volume, high-velocity, and high-variety data.
Interpretability issues arise because some models are “black boxes.”
Algorithmic bias can occur when models are trained on unrepresentative data.
Ethical considerations include consent, transparency, and accountability.
Supervised learning revolves around the use of labeled datasets—input-output pairs—enabling a model to learn underlying patterns for predictive tasks.
This paradigm mimics a teacher-student relationship, where correct outputs (labels) guide the model during training until the system can generalize its knowledge to unseen data.
The process involves mapping inputs to outputs by minimizing the discrepancy—measured by specific loss functions—between predictions and actual values.

A supervised learning workflow typically contains the following phases: data collection, labeling, data splitting (train, validate, test), model training, hyperparameter tuning, and evaluation using appropriate metrics.
Supervised learning powers tasks where the goal is to either predict discrete outcomes (classification) or continuous values (regression).
Classification tasks involve categorizing inputs into discrete classes based on their features.
Regression, by contrast, predicts continuous numeric values—for instance, forecasting housing prices or predicting patient survival time.
Some algorithms, notably decision trees and k-nearest neighbors, are versatile enough to handle both types of tasks through slight modifications in their approach.
Understanding these types is fundamental, as it governs the choice of features, model architectures, and evaluation protocols.
For instance, classification metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC) differ from those used in regression (mean squared error, R²).
Decision trees are among the most interpretable and widely adopted supervised models.
Their structure, reminiscent of a flowchart, partitions data recursively according to feature values; each internal node performs a test, and leaf nodes signify class labels or predicted values.
Constructing a tree involves selecting features that best split the data, commonly using measures such as information gain (ID3), gain ratio (C4.5), or Gini impurity (CART).
Decision trees have transparency (easy to visualize and explain), ability to handle both categorical and continuous variables, and minimal data preprocessing, but are susceptible to overfitting.
Techniques such as pruning, limiting tree depth, or ensemble approaches like random forests can mitigate these risks.

Modern decision tree implementations serve as the basis for many ensemble models (e.g., random forests, boosting), and remain crucial in sensitive domains, such as healthcare or finance, where explainability is essential.
The k NN algorithm embraces a non-parametric, instance-based approach: to predict the output for a new record, it examines the ‘k’ closest labeled examples in feature space.
k NN requires no explicit training phase, making it “lazy,” but places computational demands on prediction, especially in large datasets.
Its power lies in simplicity, adaptability to different tasks (classification, regression, anomaly detection), and resilience to noisy data when parameters are sensibly chosen.
Strengths of k NN include ease of implementation and versatility; limitations include slow prediction on large datasets and sensitivity to irrelevant features.
Logistic regression is a linear classification method that estimates the probability that an instance belongs to a class using the logistic (sigmoid) function.
Variants include binomial logistic regression for binary classification, and multinomial/ordinal logistic regression for more complex outputs.
Logistic regression assumes a linear relationship between input features and the log-odds of the outcome and requires independence among observations.
It finds broad application in domains ranging from financial risk modeling to medical diagnostics.
No single algorithm is universally superior; the optimal choice depends on problem context, data structure, and performance requirements.

Decision trees offer visibility into model logic at the expense of overfitting risk; k NN provides adaptability but struggles with large-scale data; logistic regression delivers interpretability but may underperform with complex nonlinear patterns.
Practical applications include fraud detection, image classification, handwriting recognition, and credit risk assessment.
A time series is a sequence of data points collected, recorded, or measured at successive, evenly-spaced time intervals.
Each data point represents observations or measurements taken over time, such as stock prices, temperature readings, or sales figures.

Time series data is commonly represented graphically with time on the horizontal axis and the variable of interest on the vertical axis, allowing analysts to identify trends, patterns, and changes over time.
Time series analysis enables the prediction of future trends, allowing businesses to anticipate market demand, stock prices, and other key variables.
By examining sequential data points, time series analysis helps detect recurring patterns and anomalies, providing insights into underlying behaviors and potential outliers.
By spotting potential risks, businesses can develop strategies to mitigate them, enhancing overall risk management.
Time series insights inform long-term strategic planning, guiding decision-making across finance, healthcare, and other sectors.
Time series analysis enables businesses to optimize resource allocation effectively, giving them a competitive edge.
Key properties of time series include trend, seasonality, cyclic behavior, stationarity, and autocorrelation.
Trend represents the long-term movement or directionality of the data over time, capturing increases, decreases, or stability.
Seasonality refers to periodic fluctuations or patterns that occur at regular intervals within the time series.
Cyclical variations are longer-term fluctuations without a fixed period, often associated with economic or business cycles.
Irregularity or noise refers to unpredictable fluctuations not attributable to trend, seasonality, or cycles.
Examples of time series data include weather measurements, stock prices, quarterly sales, heart rate monitoring, and EEG data.
ARIMA (Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average) models are used for forecasting time series data and combine autoregression (AR), differencing (I), and moving averages (MA).
The autoregressive part (AR) represents the dependence of the current observation on previous values.
Differencing (I) transforms a non-stationary time series into a stationary one by subtracting consecutive observations.
The moving average part (MA) indicates the dependence of the current observation on previous forecast errors.
ARIMA parameters p, d, q denote the number of AR terms, differencing order, and MA terms, respectively.
ARIMA models require stationarity, and parameters are estimated using ACF and PACF plots.
After fitting, ARIMA models can forecast future values by iterating over time.
Exponential smoothing predicts future trends by giving more weight to recent observations, assuming the future will resemble the recent past.
Simple exponential smoothing is used for time series without trend or seasonality, using a smoothing factor alpha (α).
Double exponential smoothing (Holt’s Trend Model) forecasts trends without seasonality, using alpha (α) and beta (β) to smooth level and trend.
Holt-Winters’ exponential smoothing predicts time series with both trend and seasonal components, introducing a seasonal parameter gamma (γ).
Exponential smoothing models are referred to as ETS (Error, Trend, Seasonality) models and are adjustable via smoothing parameters.
Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks are an enhanced version of RNNs designed to capture long-term dependencies in sequential data.
LSTM architectures include memory cells controlled by input, forget, and output gates, allowing selective retention or discarding of information.
LSTM applications include language modeling, speech recognition, time series forecasting, anomaly detection, recommender systems, and video analysis.
In computer engineering, time series forecasting is applied for network traffic prediction, sensor data analytics, CPU/GPU load forecasting, energy management, and control systems.
A WAN operates beyond the geographic scope of a LAN.
WANs are used to interconnect the enterprise LAN to remote LANs in branch sites and telecommuter sites.
A WAN is owned by a service provider whereas a LAN is typically owned by an organization.
An organization must pay a fee to use the WAN service provider’s network services to connect remote sites.
Service providers provide links to interconnect remote sites for the purpose of transporting data, voice, and video.
Without WANs, LANs would be a series of isolated networks.
As organizations expand, businesses require the ability to communicate between geographically separated sites.
Regional or branch offices of an organization need to be able to communicate and share data with the central site.
Organizations need to share information with other customer organizations.
Employees who travel on company business frequently need to access the corporate network.
Interconnecting multiple sites across WANs can involve a variety of service provider technologies and WAN topologies.
Point-to-Point topology employs a point-to-point circuit between two endpoints.
Hub-and-Spoke is applicable when a private network connection between multiple sites is required.
A disadvantage of the hub-and-spoke topology is that all communication has to go through the hub.
With a full mesh topology using virtual circuits, any site can communicate directly with any other site.
Dual-homed topology provides redundancy and load balancing.
This chapter will focus on a fictitious company called SPAN Engineering.
SPAN Engineering initially operates as a small office using a single LAN to share information and connect to the Internet using DSL.
Five years later, SPAN Engineering has grown into a small to medium-sized business with several hundred employees and multiple subnetworks.
Another six years later, SPAN Engineering expanded with branch offices requiring a WAN for remote sites.
SPAN Engineering has now grown to thousands of employees distributed in offices worldwide, requiring secure Internet WAN connectivity.
WAN operations focus primarily on the physical and data link layer of the OSI Model.
Data link layer requirements include physical addressing, flow control and encapsulation.
WAN access standards are defined and managed by TIA/EIA, ISO, and IEEE.
Layer 1 protocols describe how to provide electrical, mechanical, operational, and functional connections to the services of a communications service provider.
Layer 2 protocols define how data is encapsulated and the mechanisms for transferring the resulting frames.
One primary difference between a WAN and a LAN is that a company must subscribe to an outside WAN service provider to use WAN carrier network services.
Customer Premises Equipment (CPE) consists of devices and inside wiring located on the enterprise edge connecting to a carrier.
Data Communications Equipment (DCE) consists of devices that put data on the local loop.
Data Terminal Equipment (DTE) are the customer devices that pass the data from a customer network for transmission over the WAN.
Dialup modem converts digital signals to voice frequencies transmitted over the public telephone network.
Broadband modem is used with high-speed DSL or cable Internet service.
CSU/DSU are required for digital-leased lines to ensure connection integrity.
Router provides internetworking and WAN access interface ports that connect to the service provider.
Circuit-switched networks establish a dedicated circuit between nodes before communication.
The two most common types of circuit-switched WAN technologies are PSTN and ISDN.
Packet switching splits traffic data into packets that are routed over a shared network.
Connectionless systems carry full addressing information in each packet, e.g., the Internet.
Connection-oriented systems predetermine the route for a packet, e.g., Frame Relay.
Packet switching costs less than circuit switching, but latency and jitter are greater.
WAN access connection options include private and public WAN infrastructures.
Private WAN infrastructure includes leased lines, circuit-switched links, and packet-switched links.
Public WAN infrastructure includes broadband Internet using DSL, cable, or satellite.
Dense wavelength division multiplexing (DWDM) multiplies bandwidth on a single fiber and enables bidirectional communications.
T1 links support 1.544 Mb/s.
ISDN BRI provides two 64 kb/s bearer channels (B) and a 16 kb/s delta channel (D).
Frame Relay is a Layer 2 NBMA WAN technology used to interconnect enterprise LANs.
ATM uses a cell-based architecture with fixed-length 53-byte cells, suitable for voice and video traffic.
Ethernet WAN using fiber-optic cables provides reduced expenses, easy integration, and enhanced productivity.
MPLS directs data from one router to the next using labels instead of IP addresses.
VSAT creates a private WAN using satellite communications for remote locations.
DSL uses twisted-pair telephone lines to transport high-bandwidth data and requires a DSL modem.
Cable Internet uses coaxial cable to provide high-bandwidth always-on connections.
Wi MAX provides high-speed broadband coverage similar to a cell phone network rather than Wi-Fi hotspots.
Satellite Internet via VSAT provides two-way data communications, upload speed about one-tenth of download speed.
3G/4G cellular connects devices to the Internet using nearby mobile phone towers.
VPNs provide encrypted connections over public networks for teleworkers and remote offices.
Site-to-site VPNs connect entire networks; remote-access VPNs connect individual users.
A WAN is owned by a service provider and a LAN is typically owned by an organization.
Point-to-point connections connect LANs to service provider WANs and connect LAN segments.
A LAN-to-WAN point-to-point connection is also referred to as a serial connection or leased-line connection.
Lines are leased from a carrier.
Companies pay for a continuous connection between two remote sites, and the line is continuously active and available.
Serial Communication Method of data transmissions in which the bits are transmitted sequentially over a single channel.
Parallel communications Bits can be transmitted simultaneously over multiple wires.
Sends a byte (eight bits) in the time that a serial connection sends a single bit.
On most PCs, parallel ports and RS-232 serial ports have been replaced by the higher speed serial Universal Serial Bus (USB) interfaces.
Point-to-point link Used when permanent dedicated connections are required.
Provides a single, pre-established WAN communications path.
Path goes from the customer premises, through the provider network, to a remote destination.
Can connect two geographically distant sites, such as a corporate office in New York and a regional office in London.
Usually more expensive than shared services.
Constant availability is essential for some applications such as VoIP or video over IP.
Bandwidth refers to the rate at which data is transferred over the communication link.
Carrier technology will dictate how much bandwidth is available.
HDLC is a synchronous data link layer protocol developed by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO).
HDLC defines a Layer 2 framing structure that allows flow and error control through acknowledgments.
Default serial encapsulation method when connecting two Cisco routers.
Cisco’s HDLC is a point-to-point protocol that can be used on leased lines between two Cisco devices.
Protocol field makes it possible for a single serial link to accommodate multiple network-layer protocols.
Use Cisco HDLC as a point-to-point protocol on leased lines between two Cisco devices.
If connecting non-Cisco devices, use synchronous PPP.
The show interfaces serial x/x/x command displays information specific to serial interfaces.
When HDLC is configured, “encapsulation HDLC” should be reflected in the output.
“Serial 0/0/0 is up, line protocol is up”, indicates that the line is up and functioning.
PPP encapsulation should be used when there is a need to connect to a non-Cisco router.
PPP encapsulates data frames for transmission over Layer 2 physical links.
PPP establishes a direct connection using serial cables, phone lines, trunk lines, cellular telephones, specialized radio links, or fiber-optic links.
PPP contains three main components: HDLC-like framing, LCP, and NCPs.
PPP includes many features not available in HDLC: link quality management, PAP, CHAP authentication.
LCP functions within the data link layer and has a role in establishing, configuring, and testing the data-link connection.
PPP permits multiple network layer protocols to operate on the same communications link using NCPs.
A PPP frame includes Flag, Address, Control, Protocol, Data, and FCS fields.
PPP Session Phase 1: Link establishment and configuration negotiation.
Phase 2: Link quality determination (optional).
Phase 3: Network layer protocol configuration negotiation.
LCP operation includes provisions for 3 classes of LCP frames: link-establishment, link-maintenance, link-termination.
During link establishment, the initiating device sends a Configure-Request frame to the responder.
The operation of the link is handed over to the NCP.
During link maintenance, LCP can use messages to provide feedback and test the link.
After the transfer of data at the network layer completes, the LCP terminates the link.
PPP Configuration Options: Authentication (PAP/CHAP), Compression (Stacker/Predictor), Multilink to increase WAN bandwidth.
PAP is a very basic two-way process with no encryption.
CHAP is more secure than PAP and involves a three-way exchange of a shared secret.
PAP sends passwords in plaintext; CHAP conducts periodic challenges to verify the remote node.
Use the ppp authentication interface configuration command to specify PAP/CHAP order.
PPP multilink allows spreading traffic across multiple physical WAN links.
Select broadband remote access technologies to support business requirements.
Compare remote access broadband connection options for small to medium-sized businesses.
Select an appropriate broadband connection for a given network requirement.
Cable system uses a coaxial cable that carries radio frequency (RF) signals across the network.
Cable systems provide high-speed Internet access, digital cable television, and residential telephone service.
Use hybrid fiber-coaxial (HFC) networks to enable high-speed transmission of data.
Two types of equipment are required to send signals upstream and downstream on a cable system: Cable Modem Termination System (CMTS) and Cable Modem (CM).
Digital Subscriber Line (DSL) is a means of providing high-speed connections over installed copper wires.
Asymmetric DSL (ADSL) provides higher downstream bandwidth to the user than upload bandwidth.
Symmetric DSL (SDSL) provides the same capacity in both directions.
For satisfactory ADSL service, the local loop length must be less than 3.39 miles (5.46 km).
DSL connection is set up between the customer premises equipment (CPE) and the DSL access multiplexer (DSLAM) located at the Central Office (CO).
Advantage of DSL over cable technology is that DSL is not a shared medium.
Three main broadband wireless technologies: Municipal Wi-Fi, Cellular/mobile, Satellite Internet.
LTE Category 10 supports up to 450 Mb/s download and 100 Mb/s upload.
PPP can be used on all serial links including those links created with dial-up analog and ISDN modems.
Ethernet links do not natively support PPP. PPP over Ethernet (PPPoE) provides a solution to this problem.
PPPoE creates a PPP tunnel over an Ethernet connection.
MTU should be set to 1492 to accommodate PPPoE headers.
VPN is a private network created via tunneling over a public network, usually the Internet.
VPN gateway is necessary - could be a router, a firewall, or a Cisco Adaptive Security Appliance (ASA).
Benefits of VPN include cost savings, scalability, compatibility with broadband technology, and security.
Site-to-site VPNs connect entire networks, e.g., a branch office to company headquarters.
Remote-access VPN supports telecommuters and mobile users and may require VPN client software.
DMVPN is a Cisco software solution for building multiple VPNs.
GRE is a non-secure, site-to-site VPN tunneling protocol developed by Cisco.
GRE supports transportation of multiprotocol and IP multicast traffic between sites.
GRE is defined as an IETF standard (RFC 2784) and uses protocol number 47 in the IP header.
Five steps to configure a GRE tunnel: create tunnel interface, configure IP address, specify source and destination IPs, optionally specify GRE tunnel mode.
IGPs are used to exchange routing information within a company network or an autonomous system (AS).
BGP is an Exterior Gateway Protocol (EGP) used for exchange of routing information between ASs.
Every AS is assigned a unique 16-bit or 32-bit AS number.
External BGP (eBGP) is used between routers in different ASs; internal BGP (iBGP) is used within the same AS.
BGP should not be used if there is a single connection to the Internet or limited understanding of BGP.
To implement eBGP: enable BGP routing, configure BGP neighbors, and advertise networks originating from this AS.
Sample eBGP configuration: router bgp 65000; neighbor 209.165.201.1 remote-as 65001; network 198.133.219.0 mask 255.255.255.0.
Three commands to verify eBGP: show ip route, show ip bgp, show ip bgp summary.
Front-End Development is the development or creation of a user interface using some markup languages and other tools.
It is the development of the user side where only user interaction will be counted.
Front-End is a most visible, accessible and usable part of your software application.
Front-End development is one of the critical software development which will decide the success of your project.
“The purpose of front-end development is to make things that are easy to use and good to look at. - Rebecca Murphey
Programming Language Frameworks: Javascript, CSS, HTML
Responsive Web Development Methods for Achieving Responsiveness: Breakpoints, Flexible Images, Flexible Grids, Media Queries
Frontend Security: Attacks that can make your web page more risky: DoS (denial of service) attacks, Preventing cross-site request forgery (CSRF), Cross-site scripting (XSS) attacks
Back-End Development involves the logic, database, and other operations that are built behind the scenes to run the web servers efficiently.
It involves the creation and maintenance of server side logic that operates behind the scenes of web applications, APIs and other software systems.

API (Application Programming Interface) full form is an Application Programming Interface that is a collection of communication protocols and subroutines used by various programs to communicate between them.
It is code that enables two software programs to communicate.
An API facilitates the exchange of data, features and functionalities between software applications.
The working of an API can be clearly explained with a few simple steps.
An API acts as a communication medium between two programs or systems for functioning.

Steps followed in the working of APIs: The client initiates the requests via the APIs URI (Uniform Resource Identifier), The API makes a call to the server after receiving the request, Then the server sends the response back to the API with the information, Finally, the API transfers the data to the client
REST (Representational State Transfer) - Popular and widely used API architecture style. RESTful APIs uses Http request to post, create, put, update, get, read and delete.
SOAP (Simple Object Access Control) - An older technology that uses XML as its messaging format. SOAP APIs are known for their reliability and security.
GraphQL - This is a query language for API that was developed by Facebook. It enables the client to request exactly the data they need. GraphQL APIs are known for their flexibility and efficiency.

gRPC - This is a high performance open source remote procedure call framework that is widely used for building APIs. gRPC enables developers to build efficient and scalable APIs using a wide range of programming languages.
Web Sockets - This is a protocol for bidirectional real time communication between a client and the server.
Few easy steps in creating APIs: Plan your goal and the intended users, Design the API architecture, Develop (Implement the code) and Test API, Monitor its working and work on feedback
Restrictions of Using APIs: Three main types of policies governing APIs - Private, Partner, Public

Programming paradigm is an approach to solve problem using some programming language or also we can say it is a method to solve a problem using tools and techniques that are available to us following some approach.
Imperative Programming Paradigm is one of the oldest programming paradigms.
It features close relation to machine architecture.
It works by changing the program state through assignment statements.
It performs step by step task by changing state.
Advantages of Imperative Programming Paradigm: Very simple to implement, contains loops, variables etc.
Disadvantages of Imperative Programming Paradigm: Complex problems cannot be solved, less efficient and less productive, parallel programming is not possible.
Three Broad Categories of Imperative Programming: Procedural Programming Paradigm, Object Oriented Programming, Parallel Processing Approach.
Procedural Programming can also be referred to as imperative programming.
Procedural Programming emphasizes procedure in terms of underlying machine model.
Procedural Programming is excellent for general-purpose programming.
The coded simplicity along with ease of implementation of compilers and interpreters.
A large variety of books and online course material available on tested algorithms, making it easier to learn.
The source code is portable.
The code can be reused in different parts of the program, without the need to copy it.
The program flow can be tracked easily as it has a top-down approach.
Object Oriented Programming is written as a collection of classes and objects meant for communication.
In this framework, all real-world entities are represented by Classes.
Objects are instances of classes so each object encapsulates a state and behavior.
State implies the fields; attributes of the object and behavior is what you do with the state of the object and they are the methods.
Objects interact with each other by passing messages.

Features of Object Oriented Programming: Encapsulation - hide unnecessary details; Inheritance - derive a class from another; Data Abstraction - simplify representation; Polymorphism - ability to take multiple forms.
Advantages of Object Oriented Programming: Data Security, Inheritance, Code reusability, Flexibility and Abstraction.
Parallel processing is the processing of program instructions by dividing them among multiple processors.
A parallel processing system possesses many processors to run a program in less time by dividing them.
Declarative programming is a style that expresses logic of computation without talking about control flow.
Declarative Programming Paradigm is Divided into: Logic, Functional, Database.
Logic Programming Paradigm solves logical problems like puzzles and series, emphasizing knowledge base and problem; execution is like proof of mathematical statement.
Functional Programming Paradigm has roots in mathematics and is language independent; execution of series of mathematical functions is key principle.
Database / Data Driven Programming Approach is based on data and its movement; program statements are defined by data rather than hard-coding steps.
A database program is the heart of a business information system and provides file creation, data entry, update, query and reporting functions.
Programming Guidelines refer to best practices developers follow while writing code.
Guidelines ensure the code is efficient, maintainable and free of errors.
Using Pseudo Codes: Pseudo Codes is an informal language that describes steps to solve a problem or to write a program.
Benefits of Using Pseudo Codes: Improves code readability, eases maintenance, facilitates team communication.
Control Structures Guidelines are the set of rules programmers should follow to use control structures efficiently.
Documentation Guidelines include proper use of comments, clear naming conventions, meaningful function and variable names, proper use of white space.
Group of animals with spines or backbones are called vertebrates animals.
Reptiles are cold-blooded vertebrates that can also live in land and water but are more adapted to living to living on land than amphibians.
Mammals are warm blooded animals that have mammary glands used to feed their young.
Invertebrates form the greater part of animal species. They are animals without backbone.
Amphibians are cold-blooded vertebrates that can breath through their skin and lungs.
Birds are warm-blooded vertebates that have wings and covered with feathers.
Sponges or poriferans are pore-bearing, filter feeding animals.
The largest group of arthropods are the insects.
Mollusks are invertebrate animals.
Ecosystem: All the living and non-living things that interact in an area.
Food Chain: The flow of energy of one organism eating another organism.
Habitat: The place where the organism lives and provides the things that the organism needs to survive.
Decomposer organism that gets their energy by breaking down the waste and remains of dead organisms into smaller molecules.
Consumer organisms that get their food and energy by eating other organisms.
Producer organisms that produce their own food and energy.
Coneferous Forest are forest ecosystems are found in regions of the Earth that experience somewhat long and cold winters, with summer being much shorter.
Grassland are biomes are marked by sparse trees, and extensive grasses, as well as a variety of small and large animals.
Tundra are locations are known for their long cold winters, and their short cool summers.
Sepals are modified leaves which enclose and protect the other parts of a flower when it is still a bud.
Petals is the brightly colored parts that attracts insects during pollination.
Receptacle is the enlarged upper end of flower stalk which bears the flower or group of flowers.
Word is a full-featured word-processing application that allows you to create professional-looking documents and revise them easily.
This includes flyers, research papers, letters, memos, resumes, reports, mailing labels, and newsletters.
Word has many features for simplifying the design of documents.
It is very likely you will frequently interact with Microsoft Word throughout your career to create and view business reports.
Example of Word Templates (pre-prepared documents) can be found by clicking File > Home > More Templates.
Using Word Launching (= Opening/Starting) the Word App on Your Computer Use one of the following approaches, whichever works.
Fastest approach: use the “search” bar/box to search for “Word”.
On a Windows computer (including the CUNY Virtual Desktop) click the Start button and look for either of the Word icons in the Start menu.
On a Mac computer, look for either of the Word icons in the Applications folder.
Look for a Word icon on the Desktop folder of the computer.
After the Word app opens on your computer, click or double-click the Blank document option to create a new, empty Word document.
Before proceeding, make sure the window is maximized and that the document is shown in Print Layout.

Overview of Word Controls Document Window Insertion point, pointer (I-beam) Scroll Bar Scroll box, scroll up/down arrows Status Bar Current page, total # of words, spelling & grammar button, language, view options, zoom-in bar and +/- buttons.
Ribbon – the Control Center of Word Tabs (Home, Insert, Draw, Design, Layout, References, Mailings, Review, View, and Help) groups of commands.
Various items on the Ribbon: buttons, text boxes, and galleries Some allow Live Preview of the design Screen tips when hovering over buttons.
Tell Me Box (Word’s Search Box) Quick Access Toolbar Always available Can add/remove commands from it.

Saving Word Documents To save the document for the 1st time: Go to File Save As This PC, Type the name of the file in the name box, Choose the file type to be “Word Document (*.docx)” among the given choices, Click the folder icon or name to choose the folder where to save the file, Click to save the file.
Alternative: Click on the Save button on the Quick Access Toolbar and do steps 2–5 above.
To save an existing document, simply click the Save button on the Quick Access Toolbar or click Ctrl + S (Mac: Cmnd + S).
Since the advent of Word in 1983, it has been undergoing many updates.
Two Word versions are especially important to note: .doc (Word 97–2003 Document) and .docx (Word Document).
Note: “Word 97”, “Word 2003”, “Word 2007”, “Word 2013”, “Word 2016”, etc., indicate the version of the Word app on your computer.
This semester, we’ll work with .docx Word documents (create, modify, and submit as HW).
To type a letter/digit/symbol, press the key on your computer’s keyboard that corresponds to that symbol.
Press the ENTER key (RETURN on Mac) to skip to the next line.
Word-Wrap: Word continues long sentences on next line automatically, so you don’t have to click ENTER when the I-beam reaches the right edge of the page.
You can move around the document with the keyboard! Left, Right, Up, and Down arrow keys Home and End keys Ctrl shortcuts (Ctrl + Home, Ctrl + Right, etc.).
To copy, paste, cut, or apply style changes to text, you must first select it.
Word lets you select text in several ways: single line, whole paragraph, multiple lines, or several words.
After the text was selected, you can move the text by copying, cutting, or dragging.
A few extremely useful text-editing buttons are on the Home tab inside the Font group: Bold, Italics, Underline, Strikethrough, and Scripts.
The letter-casing (Change Case) button allows you to change a selected text to different casing types.
Font, Font Size, and Font Color can be found on the Home tab > Font group.
Points (pts) is the measurement unit of font sizes: about 1/72 inch in height.
Style or Theme = a pre-prepared collection of character and paragraph formatting features.
Serif fonts contain decorative spikes; Sans Serif fonts have no spikes; Monotone fonts have a robotic look.
Sans Serif fonts are easier to read on screens; serif fonts are easier to read on print text.
The Paragraph group on the Home tab contains formatting options for paragraphs.
The Align Left button aligns text to the left edge of the document window.
The Center button centers the text.
The Align Right button aligns text to the right edge of the document window.

Conformity is the tendency to change our perceptions, opinions, or behavior to match group norms.
People conform because they want to make accurate judgments of reality.
Informational influence occurs when a person believes others are correct in their judgment.
People also conform because they fear rejection.
Normative influence occurs when a person fears negative social consequences.
Informational and normative influences produce private and public conformity.
Private conformity involves true acceptance and a change in beliefs.
Public conformity is a superficial change in behavior without a change in opinion.
People tend to conform when social pressure is intense or when they are unsure how to behave.
Group size increases conformity pressure.
Social norms influence conformity only when people know and focus on them.
Having an ally makes it easier to resist conformity.
Even a single dissenter reduces pressure to conform.
Gender differences in conformity are generally weak.
Minority influence is the process by which dissenters create change.
According to Moscovici, majorities rely on numbers while minorities rely on behavioral style.
Idiosyncrasy credits are earned by following group norms.
Minority influence remains powerful.
Cultures differ in social norms and in how strongly people are expected to follow them.
Individualism prioritizes independence and self-reliance.
Collectivism prioritizes cooperation and social harmony.
Compliance refers to changes in behavior elicited by direct requests.
People can be influenced by the simple phrasing of a request.
Mindlessness can make people more vulnerable to compliance.
Mindlessness can also reduce compliance in some cases.
The norm of reciprocity dictates that we treat others as they treat us.
This norm allows retaliation against those who cause harm.
This norm obligates people to repay kindness.
Individuals who use reciprocity to elicit compliance are called creditors.
Cultures may differ in their wariness toward reciprocation.
People often find it uncomfortable to refuse unreasonable requests.
Allen Funt observed how easily people are led by authority figures.
People often follow orders from strangers showing minimal signs of authority.
Milgram conducted experiments on destructive obedience during the Eichmann trial era.
Obedience is behavior change produced by the commands of authority.
Milgram found that experimenters in lab coats were perceived as authority figures.
Physical separation from the victim increased obedience in Milgram’s studies.
Participants felt relieved of personal responsibility due to the experiment setup.
Transmitters were more obedient than executants in Kilham and Mann’s study.
Gradual escalation contributed to obedience in Milgram’s procedure.
Compliance refers to changes in behavior elicited by direct requests.
Milgram’s 1963 results showed 65% obeyed orders to inflict pain.
Meeus and Raaijmakers ordered participants to inflict psychological harm.
Groups can trigger rebellion or aggressive responses.
Social influence exists on a continuum from norms to authority commands.
Social Impact Theory explains influence through strength, immediacy, and number of sources.
Social impact is weaker when the target is strong, distant, or accompanied by others.
Conformity, compliance, and obedience can be positive or negative depending on context.
Conformity rates vary across cultures and generations.
Internet Psychology is the study of human behavior within the digital landscape of the internet and electronic communication networks.
It focuses on how people behave, feel, and interact online, integrating concepts from learning, communication, addiction, and therapy.
Online behavior is influenced by the lack of physical cues, unclear personal boundaries, and public visibility of communication.
Human behavior online is similar to face-to-face interactions but differs due to anonymity, psychological distance, and technology-mediated communication.
As more people worldwide use the internet, understanding its psychological effects is essential for addressing benefits and challenges.
In the 1990s, cyberpsychology emerged as researchers explored identity and anonymous interactions in online communities.
In the 2000s, social networks and online games grew, and psychologists studied self-presentation and online relationships.
In the 2010s, broadband and smartphones increased access to online therapy and shifted focus to digital well-being and cyberbullying.
In the 2020s, virtual reality, augmented reality, and AI expanded studies on human-AI interaction and digital adaptation.
Productive online activities reduce depressive symptoms, while excessive use may lead to social isolation or anxiety.
The internet influences how people shape identities, impacting self-esteem and social perception.
Internet psychology revolutionized mental health care through online counseling, teletherapy, and digital assessments.
Psychologists use chatbots and AI-based tools to detect mental health issues early.
The Internet of Behaviors uses online data and AI to predict human behavior.
Social media shapes emotional regulation, self-worth, and interpersonal relationships.
Internet Psychology examines how cultural and technological shifts influence communication patterns and social norms.
Behaviorism explains that addictive behaviors arise when individuals assign too much value to one activity or reward.
Online actions are learned through reinforcement, such as likes, comments, wins, or purchase rewards.
Social Learning Theory states that people learn by observing and imitating rewarded behaviors online.
Smartphone mental health apps have increased, but many lack evidence-based content.
Virtual reality effectively supports cognitive-behavioral therapy and functional recovery.
Few consumer-ready VR applications exist despite strong research support.
ChatGPT-style chatbots are increasingly used as emotional venting platforms.
Compliance refers to changes in behavior elicited by direct requests.
Teletherapy and online counseling are now widely practiced through platforms like Zoom and Google Meet.
VR exposure therapy allows patients to experience trauma-related situations safely.
Ethical challenges include privacy, informed consent, and protecting participants online.
Rapid technological change makes psychological findings quickly outdated.
Cultural bias exists in research because most studies focus on Western populations.
Personality is that which permits a prediction of what a person will do in a given situation.
Cattell’s goal was to predict how a person will behave in response to a stimulus situation.
His aim was to study personality, not treat it.
He believed it was unwise to change a personality before understanding it.
Raymond Bernard Cattell was a British-born American psychologist.
He was born on March 20, 1905, in Staffordshire, England.
He died on February 2, 1998, in Honolulu, Hawaii.
He published more than 500 articles and 43 books.
Cattell used factor analysis to identify common factors in personality data.
He defined traits as relatively permanent reaction tendencies.
Common traits are shared by all people.
Unique traits are possessed by a few individuals.
Ability traits describe skills and goal efficiency.
Temperament traits describe behavioral style.
Dynamic traits describe motivations and interests.
Surface traits correlate but do not form true factors.
Source traits are stable, permanent factors.
Constitutional traits originate biologically.
Environmental-mold traits are learned from surroundings.
Cattell proposed ergs as basic units of motivation.
A sentiment is a learned pattern of attitudes.
Cattell proposed six major stages of personality development.
Fluid intelligence involves abstract reasoning and problem solving.
Crystallized intelligence involves knowledge from past learning.
Fluid intelligence decreases with age.
Crystallized intelligence increases with age.
Fluid intelligence relates to working memory, processing speed, and reasoning.
Crystallized intelligence relates to knowledge, wisdom, and facts.
Fluid intelligence can be improved through challenge, creativity, and socializing.
Crystallized intelligence can be improved by learning and reading.
Cattell used L-data, Q-data, and T-data in personality assessment.
L-data consists of observers’ ratings of real-life behavior.
Q-data consists of self-report questionnaires.
T-data consists of objective tests measuring behavior unknowingly.
The 16 Personality Factors describe key dimensions of personality.
Cattell felt isolated because other psychologists failed to use factor analysis.
He believed his approach had the most value in studying personality.
He is considered the father of the personality trait approach and an influential 20th-century psychologist.
Instrumentation is the design, equipping, and use of measuring instruments to determine real-life conditions in a plant’s process for observation, measurement, and control.
Accuracy in instrument measurements is important for safety, product quality, and business profitability.
Industrial automation is critical in industries such as automotive, manufacturing, aerospace, pharmaceuticals, and food and beverage.
Industrial automation improves efficiency, product quality, and workplace safety by automating hazardous tasks.
Instrumentation and Control Engineering (ICE) studies the measurement and control of process variables and the systems that use them.
Instrumentation engineering focuses on designing, developing, and maintaining devices that measure and control physical variables.
Control engineering applies control theory to design systems with desired behaviors.
The purpose of instrumentation and control includes quality control, safety, and conservation of energy and materials.
Instruments are devices used to measure, monitor, or control variables such as pressure, temperature, flow, and level.
Instruments may range from simple valves to complex analyzers and often form part of control systems.
Control of processes is a main branch of applied instrumentation.
Instruments can be classified as critical, non-critical, or reference-only.
A critical instrument may compromise product or process quality if it fails to meet specifications.
An instrumentation system provides information about process variables that affect manufacturing.
Sensors are the primary elements that directly measure process variables.
Variable conversion elements convert sensor output into usable forms.
Signal processing elements improve the quality of measurement signals.
Signal transmission allows measured signals to be sent over distances.
Signal presentation or recording is where the measured signal is utilized or displayed.
Control in process industries ensures operations remain safe, stable, and within limits.
Manual control relies on human operators to monitor and adjust processes.
Automatic control uses systems such as PLCs or DCS to make real-time adjustments.
A process transforms raw materials into finished products through various operations.
Process control maintains process variables within acceptable limits for safety and quality.
A control system regulates or directs another system to achieve desired outcomes.
An open-loop control system operates without feedback.
A closed-loop control system uses feedback to maintain the set point.
An instrumentation and control loop combines instruments to regulate a process variable.
Process, measuring element, receiving element, and final control element are parts of an instrument loop.
A process variable is a measurable property such as temperature or pressure.
A set point is the desired target value of a process variable.
Controller output is the signal sent to adjust the process.
A manipulated variable is the variable adjusted to maintain the set point.
Measurement involves determining the magnitude of a variable by comparing it to a standard unit.
Measurement provides a numerical value that quantifies the variable.
Detection identifies the presence or absence of a variable without quantifying it.
An analog measuring system takes a process variable as input and converts it into a continuous analog signal.
Measuring elements must accurately represent the input variable.
A measuring element should have a proportional input–output relationship.
Measuring elements should not be affected by external factors.
Range is the extent of variation of a variable between the LRV and URV.
Span is the difference between the upper range value and lower range value.
An analog signal is continuous and can take any value within a range.
A digital signal uses binary values and operates in discrete steps.
Continuous measurement involves real-time, uninterrupted monitoring of process variables.
Sensors are the first components that detect process variables.
A transmitter converts a sensed process variable into a standardized output signal.
A transducer converts physical quantities into usable output signals.
The direct method measures a process variable directly.
The inferential method measures a variable indirectly using related variables.
Single-point measurement indicates a variable as high or low.
Continuous measurement provides ongoing variable monitoring.
Discrete measurement has binary outcomes such as true or false.
A process switch changes state based on preset conditions.
Switches have normal states classified as normally open or normally closed.
A normally-open switch completes the circuit only when actuated.
A normally-closed switch opens the circuit when actuated.
Hand switches are actuated manually.
Limit switches detect physical motion through contact.
Proximity switches detect objects without contact.
Pressure switches detect fluid pressure.
Level switches detect liquid or solid levels.
Temperature switches detect temperature using sensing elements.
Flow switches detect fluid flow through piping.
Receiving elements process signals into usable information.
Indicators display the present value of a process variable.
Recorders store historical values of process variables.
Recording helps operation guidance, troubleshooting, and management analysis.
Controllers compute corrective actions to maintain the setpoint.
PLCs are programmed computers that control processes using inputs and outputs.
DCS provides control functions and plant-wide monitoring.
SCADA systems monitor and control plants and equipment remotely.
Actuators convert controller signals into physical action.
Control valves regulate fluid flow in response to actuator signals.
Pneumatic signals typically range from 3 to 15 psi.
Electrical current signals typically range from 4 to 20 mA.
Linear interpolation is used to scale input values to output ranges.
Galileo Galilei invented the liquid-in-glass thermometer, one of the first devices to measure temperature changes by liquid expansion and contraction (1592).
Athanasius Kircher created the first mercury thermometer in 1643, offering more precise and consistent measurements.
Daniel Gabriel Fahrenheit invented the mercury thermometer and the Fahrenheit scale in 1714.
Anders Celsius proposed the centigrade (Celsius) scale in 1742, defining fixed freezing and boiling points of water.
William Thomson (Lord Kelvin) postulated absolute zero in the 1800s, forming the basis of the Kelvin scale.
Thomas Seebeck discovered the thermoelectric principle behind thermocouples in 1821.
Temperature measures the average kinetic energy of particles in a system.
Absolute zero is the minimum temperature where molecular motion ceases.
Temperature measurement (thermometry) is the process of determining local temperature for evaluation.
Temperature measurement can be classified into thermometers, probes, and non-contact methods.
Conduction transfers heat through direct contact without material movement.
Convection involves heat transfer through fluid motion driven by density differences.
Radiation transfers heat via electromagnetic waves.
The International Practical Temperature Scale (IPTS) is the basis of modern temperature measurements.
The IPTS was established in 1948, revised in 1960, and updated in 1990.
Thermocouples generate voltage proportional to temperature difference (Seebeck Effect).
RTDs measure temperature based on predictable resistance changes in metals.
Thermistors exhibit large resistance changes over narrow temperature ranges.
Pyrometers are non-contact sensors measuring thermal radiation, suitable for temperatures above 1500°C.
Radiation pyrometers detect emitted infrared energy proportional to object temperature.
Optical pyrometers compare object brightness with a calibrated filament.

Pressure is a fundamental thermodynamic parameter and is considered an intensive property.
Pressure is defined as the ratio of force applied to a unit area, represented as p = F/A.
Pressure measures how much force is exerted on a particular area.
Fluid molecules are in constant random motion (Brownian motion), causing pressure on container walls even at rest.
Static pressure refers to pressure exerted by a fluid at rest and is not influenced by motion.
Dynamic pressure is associated with fluid motion and depends on velocity and density.
Galileo Galilei discovered that water could rise only up to 10 meters in suction pumps in the late 16th century.
Evangelista Torricelli invented the barometer in 1643 and discovered atmospheric pressure and vacuum.
Blaise Pascal demonstrated that air pressure decreases with altitude.
Eugène Bourdon patented the Bourdon tube in 1849, still used today.
Pressure is measured for safety, efficiency, cost savings, and inferred measurements.
Absolute pressure is measured relative to a perfect vacuum.
Differential pressure is the pressure difference between two points.
Gauge pressure is measured relative to atmospheric pressure.
A manometer is a fluid-filled device used to measure pressure differences.
Manometers operate on the principle of hydrostatic equilibrium.
U-tube manometers measure pressure difference using liquid columns.
Well manometers measure pressure differences with negligible well rise.
Raised well manometers allow more accurate measurement of small pressures.
Sphygmomanometers are used to measure blood pressure.
Digital manometers use pressure transducers and microprocessors to display pressure.
Bellows convert pressure into mechanical motion.
Diaphragms measure pressure through membrane deflection.
Bourdon tubes measure pressure through elastic tube deformation.
Piezoresistive sensors operate based on resistance changes under force.
Differential capacitance sensors measure pressure via changes in capacitance.
Differential pressure transmitters output signals proportional to pressure difference.

Every obligation whose performance does not depend upon a future or uncertain event is demandable at once (Art. 1179).
Obligations with a resolutory condition are also demandable at once, subject to the effects of the event (Art. 1179).
An obligation payable when the debtor’s means permit is considered one with a period (Art. 1180).
A conditional obligation dependent solely on the debtor’s will is void (Art. 1182).
Impossible, unlawful, or immoral conditions annul the obligation (Art. 1183).
A condition that an event happen at a determinate time extinguishes the obligation upon expiration or impossibility (Art. 1184).
Obligations to give subject to suspensive conditions follow specific rules on loss, deterioration, or improvement (Art. 1189).
Reciprocal obligations carry the implied power of rescission in case of breach (Art. 1191).
Obligations with a period are demandable only upon arrival of the day certain (Art. 1193).
The period in an obligation is presumed for the benefit of both debtor and creditor (Art. 1196).
The debtor loses the benefit of the period under specific circumstances such as insolvency or fraud (Art. 1198).
In alternative obligations, only one prestation must be fully performed (Art. 1199).
The right of choice in alternative obligations generally belongs to the debtor (Art. 1200).
Facultative obligations exist when only one prestation is due but another may substitute (Art. 1206).
Solidary liability exists only when expressly stated or required by law or nature of obligation (Art. 1207).
Solidary creditors or debtors may act independently subject to limitations (Arts. 1212–1216).
Payment by one solidary debtor extinguishes the obligation, subject to reimbursement rules (Art. 1217).
Obligations may be divisible or indivisible depending on the nature or intent (Art. 1225).
A penal clause substitutes damages and interest unless otherwise stipulated (Art. 1226).
Courts may reduce penalties that are iniquitous or unconscionable (Art. 1229).
The nullity of the principal obligation also nullifies the penal clause (Art. 1230).

Thailand is often referred to as “The Land of Smiles.”
Thailand's capital, a fast-buzzing city of over eight million people.
The predominant religion in Thailand is Buddhism, specifically Theravada Buddhism.
Hindu practices and traditional Chinese beliefs have influenced local beliefs to an extent, with animist beliefs playing a large role in making Thai Buddhism what it is today.
The Thai language features five tones: high, mid, low, rising, and falling, each of which changes the meaning of particular ‘words’.
Written Thai is based on an alphabet adopted from the Khmers of Cambodia and is said to have become standardized during the reign of King Ramkhamhaeng during the Sukhothai period.
For example, the word "mai" can have different meanings depending on the tone.
Thailand’s national epic is a version of the Ramayana called the Ramakien.
Anumber of versions of the epic were lost in the destruction of Ayutthaya in 1767.
Three versions currently exist: one of these was prepared under the supervision (and partly written by) King Rama I.
His son, Rama II, rewrote some parts for khon drama.
The most important poet in Thai literature was Sunthorn Phu, who is best known for his romantic adventure story Phra Aphai Mani and nine travel pieces called Nirats.
Kings Rama V and Rama VI were also writers, mainly of non-fiction works as part of their programme to combine Western knowledge with traditional Thai culture.
One of the best-known classical dances is the UNESCO Heritage Khon Dance, often performed in the royal court with masked dancers and a narrator.
With a history of 400 years, the Khon dance evolved from the ritual and dance of the Hindu temple.
Lakhon Nai (court dance drama) is a dance drama with chorus singing and accompanied by a Pi Phat ensemble.
Lakhon Nai only depicts four stories -- the Ramakian, Unnarut, Dalang and Inao.
Throughout Thailand, Li-khe is a very popular folk dance performed with elaborate costumes and amusing humor.

Vietnamese literature was developed at an early date.
Despite the harsh trials of history in the form of repeated foreign invasion, its own characteristics remain.
It includes two major components which have developed simultaneously and are profoundly interrelated: Folk literature and written literature.
Vietnamese folk literature came into being very early and had a profound effect on the spiritual life of the Viet’s.
Legends, fairy tales, humorous stories, folk songs, epics and so on have a tremendous vitality and have lived on today.
First appeared around the 10th century.
From the 10th century, literary works were written in Han (classical Chinese) and chu nom.
Well-known works written in chu nom included Chinh Phu Ngam by female poet Doan Thi Diem, the Kieu story by Nguyen Du, and chu nom poems of female poet Ho Xuan Huang.
In the 20’s, and the following decades, the country’s literature was written in Vietnamese quoc ngu (Romanized national language).
Alexander de Rhodes is a French Jesuit missionary.
Traditionally, Vietnamese Literature always featured patriotism, national pride and humanism.
It was not by chance that great cultural personalities such as Nguyen Trai, Nguyen Du and Ho Chi Minh were also humanitists.
Buddhism – main religion, temples, meditation.
Confucianism – moral values, duty, respect.
Taoism – harmony with nature, balance.
Ancestor Worship – honoring the dead.

The magpie brought two matchmakers and you threw them the challenge: not less than five full quan, five thousand areca nuts, five fat pigs, and five suits of clothes.
Mother, I am twenty-three this year and still without a husband. What, Mother, dear, is your plan?
The magpie brought two matchmakers and you threw them the challenge: not less than three full quan, three thousand areca nuts, three fat pigs, and three suits of clothes.

The magpie brought two matchmakers and you threw them the challenge: not less than one full quan, one thousand areca nuts, one fat dog this time, and one suit of clothes.

Serial communication refers to the process of transmitting data in a sequential manner, one bit at a time, over a single wire or channel.
It is an important aspect of embedded systems, allowing for the exchange of information between different devices.
Asynchronous serial communication is a method of transmitting data between two devices without the need for a shared clock signal.
Instead, it uses start and stop bits to signal the beginning and end of each data packet, allowing the receiver to synchronize its timing and properly interpret the incoming data.
Common uses of asynchronous serial communication include RS-232 and UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter).
Advantages of Asynchronous Serial Communication include simplicity, long-distance communication, and flexible data rates.
Disadvantages of Asynchronous Serial Communication include higher overhead, less reliability, lower data rate, limited distance, complex error handling, and timing sensitivity.

In embedded systems, asynchronous serial communication provides a simple and efficient way for devices to exchange data without needing a shared clock, relying on start and stop bits for synchronization.
Synchronous serial communication is a method of data transfer where the transmitter and receiver share a common clock signal.
This synchronization allows data to be transmitted continuously without start and stop bits, making the process faster and more efficient compared to asynchronous communication.
Advantages of Synchronous Serial Communication include higher speed, precise timing, and support for multiple devices.
Disadvantages of Synchronous Serial Communication include complexity and synchronization issues.
Common protocols of synchronous serial communication include SPI, I2C, Ethernet, USB, CAN Bus, SD Card communication, SATA, LCD controllers, and FPGA communication.
An interrupt is a signal to the processor emitted by hardware or software indicating an event that needs immediate attention.
Interrupts can be either hardware interrupts or software interrupts.
Hardware interrupts are electronic alert signals sent to the processor from an external device, like a disk controller or an external peripheral.
Software interrupts are caused either by an exceptional condition or a special instruction in the instruction set which causes an interrupt when executed by the processor.
A microcontroller can serve devices either through interrupts or polling.
In polling, the microcontroller continuously monitors the status of the devices and serves them in a round-robin fashion, which is inefficient.
In interrupt-driven servicing, each device can get attention based on priority, and the microcontroller can ignore requests if necessary.
The program associated with an interrupt is called the Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) or interrupt handler.
To execute an interrupt, the microcontroller saves the current program counter and status, jumps to the ISR address, executes the routine, and then returns using RETI.
Interrupts can be level-triggered or edge-triggered.
Level-triggered interrupts generate an interrupt whenever the interrupt source is asserted.
Edge-triggered interrupts generate an interrupt only when they detect a change in the interrupt source signal.
Edge-triggered interrupts reduce firmware complexity and provide more flexible interrupt handling.
Time measurement refers to the process of accurately tracking, calculating, and controlling time within the context of an embedded system.
Key components of time measurement in embedded systems include clock source, timers/counters, prescaler, capture/compare modules, interrupts, real-time clock (RTC), and watchdog timer.
Methods of time measurement include event duration measurement, periodic time tracking, delay generation, and time stamping.
Time measurement can be absolute or relative, ensuring tasks are completed on time and processes are synchronized in embedded systems.
Indonesia is an archipelagic country composed of thousands of islands spread between the Indian and Pacific Oceans.
Its islands can be grouped into: 1. Greater Sunda Islands – Sumatra (Sumatera), Java (Jawa), the southern part of Borneo (Kalimantan), and Celebes (Sulawesi).
2. Lesser Sunda Islands (Nusa Tenggara) – Includes Bali and smaller islands stretching eastward through Timor.
3. Moluccas (Maluku) – Found between Celebes and New Guinea, historically called the Spice Islands.
4. Western New Guinea (Papua) – The western part of the island of New Guinea, rich in natural resources.
Jakarta, formerly known as Batavia (until 1949) and Djakarta (1949–1972), is the largest city and the capital of Indonesia.
It is located on the northwest coast of Java Island, at the mouth of the Ciliwung River, facing the Jakarta Bay of the Java Sea.
Jakarta is also known as Greater Jakarta (Jakarta Raya) and is part of the special capital district called daerah khusus ibukota Jakarta, which includes some small offshore islands in the Java Sea.
Indonesia has played a modest role in the world economy since the mid-20th century.
Despite its large size, rich natural resources, and strategic location, its economic importance has been less than expected.
However, Indonesia remains a major exporter of crude petroleum and natural gas, which contribute significantly to its national income.
The official language of Indonesia is Bahasa Indonesia (literally, “the language of Indonesia”).
It serves as the unifying language of the world’s fourth most populous country, made up of about 18,000 islands and 350 ethnic groups who speak 750 native languages and dialects.
Bahasa Indonesia, a standardized form of Malay, is the sixth most widely spoken language in the world — following Mandarin, English, Hindi, Spanish, and Arabic.
Indonesian literatures refer to the poetry and prose writings in various local languages such as Javanese, Malay, Sundanese, and others spoken by the peoples of Indonesia.
They include oral literature that was later preserved in written form, as well as modern literatures that began to appear in the early 20th century, influenced by Western culture and ideas.

Written literature in Indonesia has been preserved in the various languages of Sumatra (Acehnese, Batak, Rejang, Lampong, and Malay), in the languages of Java (Sundanese, Madurese, and Javanese), in Bali and Lombok, and in the major languages of South Celebes (Makassarese and Buginese).
Among these, the Javanese and Malay literatures are considered the most important in both quantity and quality.
Islam is the dominant religion in Indonesia, with 88% of the population identifying as Muslim.
Other religions include Hinduism, Buddhism, Confucianism, and Christianity.
Many ethnic groups continue to practice their indigenous customs, wear traditional dress, and follow animist beliefs.
For animists, gods are believed to inhabit natural features, such as Mount Agung in Bali.
Religious tolerance is common, with mottos like: “Judge not, lest ye be judged” “Do unto others as you would do to yourself”.
Indonesia has a long history of arts, dating back to the Stone Age.
Temples and architecture showcase some of the best examples, including traditional timber houses built on stilts.
Many crafts and art forms show animist traditions, tribal art, and strong Hindu and Buddhist influences.
Java: Offers a variety of crafts, including canvas art, wood carving, silverwork, clay and stone sculpture.
Java is also a major center for batik, ikat, and songket cloth, which all originate from Indonesia.
Sumatra: Known for Islamic art and architecture.
Bali and Java: Famous for wayang kulit, shadow puppets made of buffalo hide and painted.
Dance plays a major role in Indonesian culture.
Many performers train under experts from childhood.
Dance styles are expressive, precise, and often surprising.
Indonesia has over 3,000 original dances, many with tribal roots.
Bali is the best place to witness these cultural dances firsthand.
Bali’s dance dramas are often intertwined with Buddhist and Hindu mythology, such as the Ramayana and Mahabharata.
Barong & Rangda Dance: Depicts the battle of good over evil, featuring demon queens and child eaters.
Legong Dance: Considered the most graceful Balinese dance.
Topeng Dance: A masked dance that brings Balinese language to life, chronicles history and social issues, often in a comical way.
Mrs. Geni reflects on her life, love, and marriage as December arrives.
She remembers her experiences with her husband and realizes how her feelings about love have changed over time.
Though she feels both sadness and acceptance, she learns that marriage is not only about happiness, but also about understanding, sacrifice, and growing together through time.
The story reflects the Indonesian cultural belief that marriage is a lifelong duty and social expectation, especially for women.
The story shows how women were expected to be patient, loyal, and family-oriented.
Indonesians value obedience to elders and respect for tradition, even when personal happiness is at stake.
The story highlights how people, especially women, often hide their true emotions to maintain dignity and harmony.
The poem “Only in Poetry” expresses the poet’s belief that true feelings, thoughts, and beauty can only be fully expressed through poetry.
Ajip Rosidi shows that poetry is a space where one can speak freely and honestly, beyond the limits of everyday life or society.
The poem suggests that poetry gives life meaning, allowing emotions such as love, pain, hope, and longing to be understood and shared.
For the poet, only in poetry can truth and the depth of the human heart be revealed.
The poem reflects the deep respect of Indonesians for their native language and poetry as part of their cultural identity.
The poem suggests that true beauty and meaning can be found in simple, heartfelt expression, not in luxury or materialism.
Poetry is seen as a spiritual act — a way of connecting human emotion with divine or moral values.
The poem represents how art, especially poetry, keeps Indonesian traditions alive amid modernization.
The poem “The Birth of a Poem” describes how a poem is created — not suddenly, but through deep feelings, experiences, and reflections of the poet.
It shows that poetry is born from silence, pain, and the inner struggles of the heart and mind.
Subagio Sastrowardoyo explains that a poem begins as a simple thought or emotion, but grows into something powerful and meaningful through inspiration and creativity.
The poem represents the transformation of emotion into art, showing that writing poetry is both a spiritual and emotional process.
In the end, the poet reminds us that each poem carries a piece of the poet’s soul, making it alive and timeless.
The poem reflects the Indonesian value of art as a sacred process — creating a poem is seen as giving life or “birthing” something meaningful.
The poem shows that poetry comes from spiritual reflection and emotional depth, not just imagination.
Many Indonesian traditions view nature as a source of wisdom and creativity — and this poem mirrors that belief.
The poem emphasizes the value of patience and reflection before a poem is “born.”
Block Diagrams Are used to present the high-level structure of a system or process.
They show the main components or blocks and their relationship but they do not dive into the detailed sequence of the operations.
It uses blocks, lines or arrows to represent the components and stages of the system and how the components interact or related to each other.
They are used in engineering to provide an overview of how a system functions.
Simple illustration of the whole structure of a system.
Importance of Block Diagram For non-technical readers to understand the flow of the system and communicate with them easily without overwhelming technical details.
It is also the first step in designing before creating detailed diagrams that with block diagram, you can see the possible relationships and what will be the usage of the components to your system.
To help in system analysis and design by showing how components interact.
To provide a high-level overview of a system.
Key features of Block Diagram Blocks: subsystems, processes, or functions.
Arrow/lines: show direction of flow.
Labels: indication.
Types of Block Diagram Functional- shows the major function of a system and their relationship
System- represents the physical and logical arrangement of a system components
Signal flow- show the path of the signals
Process- outline operations.
Applications of Block Diagram Electonics & Electrical Engineering-circuits amplifier
Computer engineering- software process, data flow, cpu operations.
Control system- feedback loops, system dynamics, i/o relations.
Project management- visualize workflow
Industrial processes- outline steps in manufacturing or production.
Advantages of Block Diagram Simple and easy to make
Ideal for planning and troubleshooting
Easy to understand
Limations of Block Diagram Does not show the internal details and complex functionality
It needs to be accompanied with diagrams that can show the complex details like flowcharts, schematics, circuit,

(it is a digram that shows the step of a process using shapes and arrow.)
Detailed view of how a process works, including the order of operations and decision points.
Symbol in Flowchart Oval- terminator(Start or end)
Rectangle- process
Diamond-decision
Parallelogram- data entry(input, display (output))
Arrow-direction of flow
Circle- connector

Program-design algorithm
System-illustrates how data flow
Data-flow of information rather than operation
