Singapore Island was first inhabited by fishermen and pirates.
It served as an outpost of the Sumatran Srivijaya Empire.
In Javanese inscriptions and ancient Chinese records (14th century), the island was called Tumasik / Temasek.
The name comes from the Javanese word tasek, meaning “sea.”
1025: Rajendra Chola I, ruler of the South Indian Chola Kingdom, attacked the island.
1068: Another Chola raid occurred.
1275: The Javanese king Kertanagara likely attacked Temasek during his raid on Pahang, located on the east coast of the Malay Peninsula.
Singapore is a city-state located at the southern tip of the Malay Peninsula.
It is about 85 miles (137 km) north of the Equator.
The country is made up of the diamond-shaped main island (Singapore Island) and around 60 smaller islets.
The main island covers almost the entire land area, with only about 18 square miles belonging to the smaller islands.
Singapore has four official languages: Malay, Mandarin, Tamil, and English.
Because of its bilingual education policy, most Singaporeans speak two or more languages — usually English, their ethnic mother tongue, and sometimes other languages.
Singapore’s religious culture is very diverse, just like its population.
From a young age, Singaporeans learn about different religious customs and traditions.
Public holidays include Christian, Muslim, Hindu, and Buddhist celebrations.
Singapore is home to ten major religions, including Buddhism, Taoism, Islam, Hinduism, and Christianity.
Some people even practice or embrace more than one faith.
However, religious discrimination can still occur at times.
Singaporean literature refers to the body of literary works created by Singaporeans.
These works are written in the country’s four main languages: English, Malay, Chinese, and Tamil.
Singapore’s multicultural society has greatly influenced its literature, resulting in diverse themes and styles.
Notable writers such as Tan Swie Hian and Kuo Pao Kun have produced works in multiple languages, showcasing Singapore’s linguistic richness.
Singapore is a small island nation but home to four major ethnic groups: Ethnic Chinese, Malays, Ethnic Indians, Eurasians (small community).
Despite its size, Singapore is a multiracial and multicultural society.
This cultural diversity gives rise to a wide range of traditional ethnic clothing that represents and shapes the identity of the nation.
Exploring the traditional dress of each ethnic group helps us better understand Singapore’s rich cultural heritage.
“Grandfather’s Story” is about a narrator who remembers her grandparents’ troubled relationship.
The grandmother was a powerful but cruel businesswoman who mistreated her bondmaids.
Because of her harshness, the grandfather disliked her deeply, and they lived separately for many years.
After the grandfather’s beloved mistress dies, he becomes sad and withdrawn.
One day, he tells the narrator a long story about a farmer, his kind wife, and a wicked nun.
In the story, karma punishes the cruel and rewards the good, showing that actions have consequences even after death.
Through this tale, the grandfather hints at his own life — the suffering caused by cruelty and the hope that justice comes in the end.
The poem “Sunflowers for a Friend” speaks to a friend (or children) who have suffered verbal and emotional abuse, especially from their parents.
The speaker remembers how painful it is to hear cruel, harsh words — not just insults, but “gross epithets” like “cursed … damned reprobate.”
Despite this, there’s a refusal to be broken: the speaker acknowledges human faults and sins (“transgressors of her law”), yet still holds on to hope and seeks “grace,” even if it seems random.
She reflects on how people don’t choose their birth or early circumstances — some are stunted, warped, or even die despite loving care.
But she is amazed at how sunflowers grow from dirt.
This becomes a powerful metaphor: like sunflowers, the abused friend should keep their inner strength (“inner orientation”) and always turn toward the light, even after a dark night.
Laos promotes Peace, Independence, Democracy, Unity, and Prosperity.
Laos is a landlocked country in mainland Southeast Asia.
It is located in the northeast-central part of the region.
The country is shaped like an irregular round area in the north that narrows to the southeast.
It stretches about 640 miles from northwest to southeast.
The capital city is Vientiane along the Mekong River.
Laos is known as the Land of a Million Elephants, derived from its ancient name Lan Xang.
Laos shares boundaries with China, Vietnam, Cambodia, Thailand, and Myanmar.
The official language of Laos is Lao.
French was once used by the upper classes and in the cities.
English began replacing French around the 1970s.
Vietnamese became a third language of the elite under the Lao People’s Revolutionary Party.
Lao literature has a rich oral tradition of poetry and folk tales.
The earliest written works appeared in the 16th century during the Lan Xang period.
Early literature was used to teach Buddhist values and proper behavior.
Lao poetic traditions may have begun as early as the 14th century.
In the 19th century, literature reflected social and political changes in the region.
Theravada Buddhism is the major religion in Laos.
Buddhism was the state religion during the prerepublic kingdom.
About half of the Lao population are Buddhist.
Around two-fifths of the population follow local or traditional religions.
The Tak Bat ritual is a sacred Buddhist practice in Laos.
Every morning, monks walk barefoot to collect alms.
The collected food feeds the poor, serves as the monks’ only meal, and is shared with temple animals.
The ritual is especially famous in Luang Prabang.
Laotian food is known for being vibrant, colorful, and flavorful.

Analytical listening, sometimes called critical or active listening, deals with one’s ability and capacity to carefully and properly analyze the sound/s listened to.
Stages of Listening refers to the actual hearing process.
In here, the listener has to check him/herself if he/she is able to hear clearly the sounds and other sound points.
Receiving Stage focuses on generating meaning on what has been heard.
Understanding Stage requires both the listener and the speaker to meet in between regarding the points portrayed in the listening process.
This allows the listener to critically examine the details of the information heard.
Evaluating Stage allows the listeners to provide verbal and/or non-verbal feedback and responses based on the listening contexts.
Responding Stage is a personal stage for the listener allowing him/her to integrate into him/herself the information heard.
This also allows him/her to record in his/her system the information listened to for future access and use.
Remembering Stage

An argument is sometimes called persuasive writing and seeks to convince readers to support a stand on a certain issue.

Lesson 5 is about defining multimodal elements and its five modes.
Students should classify examples of multimodal texts according to their type.

The five modes include linguistic, gestural, visual, spatial, and audio.
Examples of linguistic modes include speeches, essays, slogans, and captions.
Examples of visual modes include cartoons, posters, photos, and infographics.
Examples of audio modes include podcasts, radio ads, songs, and background music.
Examples of gestural modes include dance performances, stage plays, facial reactions, and gestures.
Examples of spatial modes include brochure design, website layout, and seating arrangement.
TikTok dance challenges primarily use the gestural mode.
News articles primarily use the linguistic mode.
Radio commercials primarily use the auditory mode.
Seating arrangement primarily uses the spatial mode.
Billboards primarily use the visual mode.

Organisms such as humans and almost all animals have a complete digestive system with a mouth and an anus.
More primitive animals like the hydra have an incomplete digestive system with only one opening.
Nutrient deficiency happens when the body does not receive or absorb the necessary amount of nutrients from food.
Iron deficiency occurs when there is insufficient iron in the body, causing anemia.
The body cannot produce enough red blood cells during iron deficiency, leading to tiredness and shortness of breath.
Iron-deficiency anemia is commonly treated with iron supplements that restore iron levels within three to six months.
Zinc deficiency occurs when the body does not receive enough zinc, leading to appetite loss and poor immune function.
Iodine deficiency happens when the body lacks enough iodine, causing metabolic problems such as goiter.
Iodine deficiency is treated with iodine supplements taken orally.
Digestive diseases occur when harmful foods upset the gastrointestinal tract.
Constipation is a condition involving uncomfortable or infrequent bowel movements.
Diarrhea is the condition of having at least three watery bowel movements per day.
Peptic ulcers occur when the stomach or intestinal lining becomes damaged.
Colon cancer is a growth of cells beginning in the colon.
Appendicitis is inflammation of the appendix attached to the large intestine.
GERD is a digestive disorder caused by stomach acid flowing back into the esophagus.

Star formation theory states that stars are formed when a dense region of molecular cloud collapses.
During this process, fragments of clouds contract and form a stellar core known as protostar.
The contraction and gravitational force of the protostar result in an increase in temperature which triggers nuclear reaction within the star upon reaching 10 million Kelvin.
Throughout the reaction, neutrinos and positrons are released, slowing down the reaction.
Once the contraction stops and the protostar attains its gravitational equilibrium, a main sequence star will be formed.
In the core of a main sequence star, hydrogen fuses with helium through proton-proton chain.
The gravitational force of a main sequence star forces hydrogen and helium to fuse resulting to burning of the 2 primordial elements.
At this stage, helium is converted to carbon at the core while hydrogen is converted into helium surrounding the core which denotes the formation of red giant.
Massive stars undergo a series of stages where heavier elements are fused around the shell of the core.
Carbon will be formed through helium fusion, neon through oxygen fusion, magnesium from neon fusion, silicon from magnesium fusion, and iron from silicon fusion.
The rate of reaction decreases when helium is converted into carbon, causing gravity to squeeze the entire star.
Low-mass stars with insufficient energy blow off their outer material, leaving an inert carbon core forming a white dwarf.
The first 3 minutes of the Big Bang focused on expansion, cooling, and synthesis of the first three elements.
Stellar nucleosynthesis forms heavier elements such as Beryllium and Iron by combining protons and neutrons of lighter nuclei.
Heavier elements are created in different types of stars as they die or explode, and abundances change as stars evolve.
Stable Helium reacts with Carbon to produce Oxygen and gamma rays under extreme conditions.
Oxygen reacts with Helium to form Neon and gamma rays.
Carbon reacting with Carbon produces Magnesium and gamma rays.
Oxygen to Oxygen fusion produces Silicon, a Helium nucleus, and gamma rays.
Fusion processes continue to create heavier elements but not heavier than Iron.
A supernova occurs when the core can no longer produce enough energy to resist gravitational force.
Interstellar dust and gases support stellar nucleosynthesis and star formation theory.
Infrared radiation emitted during stellar formation indicates that nucleosynthesis occurs concurrently with star formation.
Henry Moseley arranged elements in the periodic table using atomic number as basis.
He emphasized that adding a proton increases atomic number and forms a new element.
Alpha emission releases a particle with two protons and two neutrons, forming a lighter element.
Beta emission occurs when a neutron becomes a proton and an electron is ejected.
Gamma emission releases a gamma ray when a radioactive nucleus leaves an excited state.
Polarity refers to having a dipole, that is, a positive and a negative end.
Polarity is dictated by the distribution of electrons in the molecule.
Molecules can be classified as polar or nonpolar.
Polarity affects and influences the behavior of a molecule.
The polarity of a molecule can be predicted from its shape.
When electrons in a polar covalent bond are unequally shared, partial charges form.
In hydrochloric acid, electrons are more concentrated on chlorine due to higher electronegativity.
This charge separation is responsible for polarity.
Dipole moment describes the polarity of a bond in a molecule.
If dipole moments do not cancel out, the molecule is polar.
Polar molecules result from polar bonds arranged asymmetrically.
If dipole moments cancel, the molecule is nonpolar.
Carbon tetrachloride is nonpolar due to symmetrical tetrahedral arrangement.
Solubility is the ability of a substance to dissolve in another.
Miscibility refers to the ability of two liquids to mix in all proportions.
Polarity directly affects solubility and miscibility.
Polar liquids mix with polar liquids; nonpolar with nonpolar.
Like dissolves like and like mixes with like.
Vinegar mixes well with water because both are polar.
Boiling point is the temperature at which a liquid becomes gas.
Polarity influences boiling point.
More polar molecules have stronger attraction and higher boiling points.
Water has strong hydrogen bonds, giving it a high boiling point.
Nonpolar molecules have lower boiling points due to weaker attractions.
An intermolecular force of attraction (IMFA) is simply an attractive force between neighboring molecules.
There are three common types, namely: London dispersion forces, dipole-dipole, and hydrogen bonds.
London dispersion forces are present in molecules, regardless of the polarity.
This is the weakest among the intermolecular forces.
They originate from the fluctuations of the electron distribution around the molecule over time.
Since larger molecules have larger electron clouds, they exhibit stronger dispersion forces than smaller molecules.
Dipole-dipole forces are present only in polar molecules.
This kind of force is stronger than London dispersion forces because polar molecules have a permanent uneven distribution of electrons.
Hydrogen bond is a special type of dipole-dipole interaction that occurs in molecules having a hydrogen atom bonded to an electronegative atom such as fluorine, oxygen, or nitrogen.
It is the strongest intermolecular force of attraction among the three.
Methane (CH₄) is a non-polar molecule that contains only London dispersion forces.
Octane (C₈H₁₈) also exhibits only London dispersion forces.
Hydrogen chloride (HCl) shows dipole-dipole attraction because of the electronegativity difference between hydrogen and chlorine.
Water (H₂O) exhibits hydrogen bonding because hydrogen is bonded to a highly electronegative oxygen atom.
Intermolecular forces are involved in phase changes.
The IMFA in solids are very strong, thus the particles are compact.
The IMFA in liquids are not strong enough to keep particles fixed in position.
The IMFA in gases are extremely weak or almost negligible.
The intermolecular forces are associated with the observable properties of various substances.
The physical properties of molecules depend upon the type and strength of their intermolecular forces of attraction.
These properties are solubility, melting point, boiling point, surface tension, viscosity, capillary action, and evaporation rate.
The solute and the solvent mix when they both exhibit the same intermolecular forces of attraction.
Substances with stronger IMFA have higher melting and boiling points.
Molecules with stronger IMFA exert greater cohesive forces and higher surface tension.
Molecules with stronger IMFA have higher viscosity.
Stronger IMFA lead to better capillary action.
A lower evaporation rate indicates weaker intermolecular forces.
Living organisms are primarily made up of organic compounds.
The process of building organic molecules by living organisms is called biosynthesis.
Biomolecules (also known as macromolecules) are organic compounds that make up all living organisms.
Organic compounds are carbon-rich molecules usually in association with hydrogen and oxygen.
The four macromolecules are carbohydrates, lipids, nucleic acids, and proteins.
Carbohydrates are one of the most abundant macromolecules on Earth.
Carbohydrates have a 1:2:1 ratio of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen.
Carbohydrates are classified as simple or complex.
Lipids are insoluble in water but soluble in nonpolar solvents.
Lipids have three major functions: energy storage, structural, and chemical messengers.
Fats are composed of long-chain fatty acids bonded to glycerol.
Unsaturated fats have at least one double bond and are liquid at room temperature.
Saturated fats have no double bonds, are solid at room temperature, and are present in animal fats.
Nucleic acids store genetic information and exist as DNA or RNA.
Nucleotides are composed of a pentose sugar, phosphate group, and nitrogenous base.
Proteins are complex macromolecules with 20 known amino acids as building blocks.
Each amino acid has an amino group, carboxylic group, and a unique side chain (R group).
Proteins are classified by function: structural, regulatory, contractile, protective, transport, and storage.
Molecules that make up a living organism are called biomolecules.
Biomolecules are produced through biosynthesis.
Carbohydrates literally mean “hydrates of carbons.”
Lipids include fats and oils, also known as triglycerides.
Hydrogenation of oils produces trans fats.
DNA carries genetic makeup; RNA is for protein synthesis.
Examples of proteins: keratin, insulin, actin, myosin, antibodies, hemoglobin, egg albumin.
Biological macromolecules are large molecules that are important components of living systems.
Performing a variety of functions, they are vital to sustaining life.
The term “macromolecule” was coined in 1920 by Nobel Laureate Hermann Staudinger.
Staudinger was also the first to assert that large biological molecules are made up and formed by covalently bonded sub-units.
Macromolecules perform a crucial role in our cell structure and processes.
Majority of biological macromolecules are polymers and are made of covalently linked basic units called monomers.
All the monomers which make up a polymer are typically similar or highly similar to one another and are bonded to form a larger macromolecule.
Monomers that link with other monomers in various combinations result in the formation of polymers with different structures and functions.
Carbohydrates are composed of carbon and hydrate and are classified as simple or complex sugars.
Carbohydrates serve as a primary source of energy, storage of energy, structural support for cells, and food.
Proteins are polymers of amino acids arranged in a linear sequence with a central carbon atom attached to functional groups.
Proteins are the most abundant organic molecules in living systems and have diverse functions including regulatory, structural, protective, contractile, transport, and storage roles.
Lipids are hydrophobic, nonpolar molecules containing carbon–hydrogen bonds and are insoluble in water.
Lipids include fats, oils, waxes, phospholipids, and steroids and perform functions such as energy storage, insulation, hormone building, and forming plasma membranes.
Nucleic acids are biological macromolecules vital in the continuity of life.
Nucleic acids carry the genetic material of a cell and provide instructions for cellular functioning.
There are two main types of nucleic acids: DNA and RNA.
DNA carries genetic material found in all living organisms.
As an engineering student, you need to develop good written and oral communication skills.
You need to know how to express your thoughts, present a concept for a product or a service, and present an engineering analysis of a problem and its solution.
You need to know how to communicate design ideas by means of engineering drawings or computer-aided modeling techniques.

Most engineers are required to write reports that may be detailed and lengthy, may contain charts, graphs, and engineering drawings, or may be brief in the forms of progress reports, memorandum, or executive summary.
Problem Solving involves 4 basic steps: defining the problem, simplifying the problem, performing the solution or analysis, and verifying the results.
Next slide: Homework examples
In defining the problem, understand what is being asked, what is known, and what is to be determined.
In simplifying the problem, make appropriate assumptions and understand the fundamental concepts, physical laws, and their limitations.
You can ask your professor if you’re unsure about the homework format.
In performing the solution or analysis, select appropriate models, apply mathematical concepts, physical laws, and engineering fundamentals, and show appropriate units.
In verifying the results, check if the answer makes sense, know the range of answers, and understand other ways to analyze the problem.
Homework presentations should include the problem statement, extracted information, what is to be determined, solution, appropriate models, and step-by-step calculations.
Progress reports are a short form of communication to convey work completed, work expected, and any problems with recommended solutions.
Executive summaries are brief forms of communication to top management to convey the findings of a study or proposal.

Technical reports should include title, abstract, objectives, theory & analysis, apparatus & experimental procedures, data & results, discussion, conclusions & recommendations, references, and appendix.

Design reports should include title, executive summary, design objectives, design assumptions, limitations & specifications, design summary, design drawings, alternative designs, economic analysis, conclusions & recommendations, references, and appendix.

Military Science (MS-1) Basic Intelligence.
For the purpose of this study, let us look into the importance of intelligence as visualized by Sun Tzu, a Chinese military philosopher and thinker.
He said: “If you know the enemy and know yourself, you need not fear the result of a hundred battles.”
“If you know yourself but not the enemy, for every victory gained you will also suffer a defeat.”
“If you know neither the enemy nor yourself, you will succumb in every battle.”
Intelligence is knowledge acquired by the collection, evaluation, analysis, integration, and interpretation of information concerning an enemy.
Counter-intelligence includes measures to safeguard information, personnel, and materials from espionage, subversion, and sabotage.
The intelligence cycle is a continuous process with no first or last phase.
The five phases of the intelligence cycle are planning, collection, processing, analysis, and dissemination.
Planning determines intelligence requirements and assigns collection agencies.
Intelligence requirements include enemy capabilities, vulnerabilities, weather, terrain, and order of battle.
Preventing surprise and supporting future decisions are also part of planning.
Intelligence priorities include Essential Elements of Information (EEIs).
Other Intelligence Requirements (OIRs) are also identified during planning.
A collection agency is an individual or unit that systematically exploits sources of information.
Categories of collection agencies include intelligence specialists, troops, and special units.
Collection of information is the systematic extraction of information from sources.
Processing converts information into intelligence through recording, evaluation, and interpretation.
Evaluation determines reliability, credibility, and value of the information.
Interpretation determines the significance of information and forms conclusions.
Dissemination involves transmitting intelligence to higher, lower, and adjacent units.
Timeliness and propriety are key criteria in dissemination.
Methods of dissemination include personal contact, messages, and intelligence documents.
CDI 101: Fundamentals of Criminal Investigation and Intelligence.
For educational purposes only.
Arrest.
Rule 113, Rules of Court.
Based on Section 1, Rule 113 of the Rules of Court, arrest is the taking of a person into custody in order that he may be bound to answer for the commission of an offense.
A warrant of arrest is a document from the court ordering any law enforcer to apprehend and detain a person named in the warrant to answer matters regarding a crime.
A warrantless arrest is an arrest made by any law enforcer without the benefit of a warrant.
Arrest without warrant is lawful when the person has committed, is committing, or attempting to commit an offense in the presence of the arresting officer.
This is known as in flagrante delicto.
Arrest without warrant is also lawful when an offense has just been committed and there is probable cause based on personal knowledge to believe that the person committed it.
This is known as hot pursuit.
Arrest without warrant is lawful when the person is an escapee from a penal establishment or confinement.
Rules on making arrest require actual restraint or submission to custody.
The arresting officer should not use unnecessary force.
Only necessary force should be used to arrest a person.
Execution of a warrant means carrying out the duty to find the person named in the warrant and cause his arrest and detention.
A warrant may be executed during day or night.
The person making the arrest must inform the person arrested that a warrant has been issued.
A warrant must be executed within ten days from the time it was received.
Officers may proceed with an arrest without informing the person of the cause of arrest if the person flees.
Officers may proceed if the person forcibly resists.
Officers may proceed if informing the person will hamper the arrest.
The officer must report to the judge reasons for failure to arrest within the period.
Search and seizure.
A warrant simply means a permit given by the court for a particular purpose.
A search is a form of government invasion of a person’s privacy.
Seizure is the confiscation of property or materials related to illegal activities to serve as evidence.

A search warrant is an order in writing issued in the name of the People of the Philippines, signed by a judge and directed to a peace officer to search for personal property described and bring it before the court.
The same definition applies under Section 1, Rule 126.
A search warrant application may be filed in any court within the territorial jurisdiction where a crime was committed.
It may also be filed in any court within the judicial region for compelling reasons.
If the criminal action is already filed, the application must be made in the court where the action is pending.
A judge issues a warrant after questioning the complainant and possible witnesses under oath.
The place to be searched must be described along with the properties to be seized.
The sworn statement must be recorded with the affidavit.
Once probable cause is established, a warrant may be issued.
Probable cause means there is a strong ground that a crime has been committed or is about to be committed and that evidence is in the place described.
Personal property to be seized may be the subject of the offense.
It may be stolen, embezzled, or fruits of the offense.
It may be used or intended to be used in committing an offense.
The officer must identify himself and his purpose before conducting the search.
If he is not admitted into the premises, he may break any part of the house to execute the warrant.
He may also break into the premises to liberate himself or anyone aiding him when unlawfully detained.
The warrant is valid only for ten days from the date of issuance.
No search shall be made except in the presence of the lawful occupant.
A member of the occupant’s family may also be present during the search.
At least two witnesses of sufficient age and discretion from the same locality must be present if the lawful occupant is absent.
The general rule is that search warrants must be executed during daytime unless specified otherwise.
The officer seizing property must give a detailed receipt to the lawful occupant.
If the occupant is absent, the officer must leave a receipt in the presence of two qualified witnesses.
The officer must deliver the seized property to the issuing court along with an inventory under oath.
If the warrant is not returned within ten days, the issuing judge shall summon the officer to explain.
The return of the warrant must be filed and kept by the custodian of the search warrant logbook.
The logbook must contain the date of return.
It must contain the result of the search.
It must contain the action of the judge.
Warrantless searches include search incidental to lawful arrest.
Warrantless searches include seizure in plain view.
Warrantless searches include stop and frisk.
Warrantless searches include consented searches.
Warrantless searches include search of a moving vehicle.
Warrantless searches include searches based on tipped information.
Search warrantless search.

Voluntary surrender has value in the prosecution of a crime.
There are requisites of voluntary surrender.
When investigating a crime, if a person voluntarily surrenders claiming to be the perpetrator, police officers must follow proper procedure.
Surrender is considered voluntary only when certain conditions are met.
There is a test of spontaneity in determining voluntary surrender.
Police officers may take custody of a person who voluntarily surrendered and may detain him following legal procedures.
Extra-judicial confession refers to an admission of guilt made outside the courtroom.
Custodial investigation refers to questioning initiated by law enforcement after a person is taken into custody.
Essential requisites are needed for a situation to fall under custodial investigation.
A police officer should not accept an oral extra-judicial confession during custodial investigation.
There is a difference between an extra-judicial confession and a judicial admission.
