Polygraphy For Educational Purposes Only.
Polygraph is a scientific instrument used as an aid in detecting deception.
It is sometimes called a lie detector.
It is defined as the scientific method of detecting deception done with the aid of a polygraph machine.
The person who conducts the polygraph examination can determine if the subject is truthful or deceitful through the aid of the polygraph machine.
The subject is the person who is placed under polygraph examination.
Physiological changes are body changes occurring when a person is performing an act or under stress, such as sweating, faster heartbeat, and heavy breathing.
Pneumograph, galvanometer, and blood pressure cuff are included in the cardiosphygmograph.
In a polygraph examination, the subject will be under stress if he intends to lie, resulting in physiological changes recorded and interpreted by the examiner.
Reading a polygraph chart requires formal training, but understanding basic principles allows distinguishing truths from lies.
Significant changes such as faster heart rate, higher blood pressure, or increased perspiration indicate lying.
The polygraph machine cannot tell whether a person is lying and only serves as an aid in investigation if the person agrees to undergo polygraph examination.
Spiral search method is best for small, circular crime scenes where there are no physical barriers.
Wheel or ray method is best for small, circular crime scenes where there are no physical barriers.
Line, strip, or parallel method is suited for scenes like classrooms with rows, and is best in large scenes but requires many people.
Double strip or grid method is effective but very time consuming.
Quadrant or zone method is most effective in houses or buildings based on floor plans.
Philosophy is the study of knowledge and wisdom from its Latin roots philo (love) and sophia (wisdom).
Philosophy is regarded as the Queen of All Sciences because all scientific disciplines rest on philosophical foundations.
Philosophers throughout history sought to explain natural causes of everything, including the nature of the self.
Greek philosophers rejected myths and used rational questioning to understand reality and the self.
The lecture introduces different philosophical perspectives about the self.
Socrates was a philosopher from Athens whose ideas influenced European thought.
Socrates did not write his teachings and is known through Plato’s writings.
Socrates developed the Socratic Method, a style of questioning to reach correct definitions.
The Socratic Method involves dialogue to clarify ideas and find logical consistency.
Socrates’ philosophy centers on the Delphic command “Know Thyself.”
Socrates taught that self-knowledge goes beyond the physical body.
Socrates believed the self is composed of body and soul.
The body belongs to the physical realm which is changeable and imperfect.
The soul belongs to the ideal realm which is unchanging and immortal.
For Socrates, the self is the soul, an immortal and unified entity consistent over time.
Plato, a student of Socrates, proposed a three-part soul: reason, appetite, and spirit.
Reason seeks truth and makes wise decisions.
Appetite consists of biological needs.
Spirit refers to basic emotions and drive.
Plato said reason must control appetite and spirit for harmony.
Plato illustrated the soul through the chariot metaphor in “Phaedrus.”
Successful charioteers reach wisdom; unsuccessful ones face failure.
St. Augustine integrated Plato’s ideas with Christian theology.
Augustine taught that the body and soul are united but distinct.
Augustine identified two realms of human nature: God as truth and the sinfulness of man.
Eternal truths are known through God.
Sin results from human free will and moral goodness requires God’s grace.
Real happiness is found in loving God above all.
René Descartes introduced “Cogito, ergo sum” (“I think, therefore I exist”).
Descartes held that true knowledge requires doubting everything.
The essence of self is being a thinking thing.
Descartes argued the mind is non-material and independent from the body.
John Locke described the mind as a “tabula rasa” or blank slate shaped by experience.
Locke defined the self as consciousness.
Personal identity is based on memory, not substance.
David Hume argued that there is no permanent self.
Hume stated that humans only experience impressions and ideas.
Hume claimed the self is a fictional construct.
Sigmund Freud developed a dualistic model of conscious and unconscious self.
The conscious self follows the reality principle.
The unconscious self follows the pleasure principle.
Freud’s structural model consists of the id, ego, and superego.
Human behavior is driven by life instincts (eros) and death instincts (thanatos).
Gilbert Ryle argued that the self is a pattern of behavior, not a separate mental entity.
Ryle rejected dualism and called it a category mistake.
Immanuel Kant said the self synthesizes sensations into meaningful experience.
The self is not directly experienced but organizes consciousness (transcendental apperception).
Kant taught that the true nature of things (noumena) is unknowable.
Paul and Patricia Churchland claimed the self is a product of brain activity.
Churchland argued that mental states are reducible to brain states.
Eliminative materialism seeks a new vocabulary grounded in neuroscience.
Maurice Merleau-Ponty believed mind and body are unified.
The self is shaped through perception and interaction with the world.
Consciousness, world, and body are interconnected.
Perception involves both sensing and interpretation.
Understanding the self in different classifications of physical aspects is one way to discover a person’s real self.
The understanding of the Physical Self is shaped by biological and environmental factors.
Heredity is defined as the transmission of traits from parents to offspring.
Genotype refers to the specific information embedded within one’s genes; not all genotypes translate to an observed physical characteristic.
Phenotype is the physical expression of a particular trait.
Each individual carries 23 pairs of chromosomes, which are threadlike bodies in the nucleus of the cell and the storage unit of genes.
The 23rd pair, also known as sex chromosomes, determines the sex of an individual.
Within each chromosome is DNA, which contains the genetic instructions specifying the biological development of every individual.
Maturation is the completion of growth of a genetic character within an organism or the unfolding of an individual’s inherent traits or potential.
As you grow up, environmental influences shape your physical self, including social networks, societal expectations, and cultural practices.
Self-understanding is the individual’s cognitive representation of self which consists of substance and self-conceptions.
Self-understanding changes throughout the lifespan as a person grows older.
An obvious change in teenagers is the change in Physical Self.
The Physical Self refers to the concrete dimensions of the body, which can be directly observed and examined.
Physical Characteristics are defining traits or features of a person’s body.
Erik Erikson believed the body and intellectual skills serve as a basis for a sense of competence.
William James considered the body as the initial source of sensation and necessary for personality.
Puberty is a period of rapid physical changes and is a marker of the beginning of adolescence.
Girls reach puberty earlier and experience menarche (first menstrual flow), while boys experience spermarche (first ejaculation) later.
Puberty is a brain neuroendocrine process that triggers rapid physical changes.
The Pituitary gland controls growth and regulates functions of other endocrine glands including gonads.
Gonadal hormones promote physical changes like breast development in girls, body hair, muscles, and voice changes in boys.
The relationship between self and body can be positive, negative, helpful, or destructive.
Freud’s psychodynamic perspective emphasizes the body in understanding the self.
The development of a body self has three stages: early psychic experience, defining body surface boundaries, and distinguishing internal states.
Physical body has both objective and subjective components.
Cognitive-behavioral perspective focuses on perceptual, cognitive, and affective aspects of body image.
This perspective distinguishes historical versus proximal determinants of body image that influence thoughts, feelings, and behaviors.
Feminist perspective emphasizes social construction and women’s dissatisfaction with their bodies as a societal phenomenon.
Western duality of mind and body pairs men with mind and women with body, affecting women’s experience of their bodies.
Personal factors affecting Physical Self include introspection, self-perception, self-concept, and personal identity.
Introspection is observing and examining one’s internal state after behaving in a certain way.
Self-perception theory explains that people infer their inner states by observing their own behavior.
Self-concept is a cognitive representation of self-knowledge including beliefs, roles, values, goals, and fears.
Physical concept is the individual’s perception or description of their physical self, including appearance.
Personal identity includes aspects of life such as family, nationality, gender, traits, occupation, friends, and beliefs.
Social factors include attachment processes, social appraisal, and maintaining the self in relationships.
The Looking-glass Self theory states that self-image is shaped by how others perceive and judge the individual.
Social comparison involves evaluating oneself by comparing traits, abilities, or opinions with others.
Upward social comparison happens when comparing oneself to someone better off.
Downward social comparison happens when comparing oneself to someone worse off.
Social Identity Theory states that understanding oneself comes from being a member of a group.
Membership in groups enhances self-esteem by providing security and belonging.
Social groups include gender, ethnicity, religion, profession, political membership, and business organizations.
Understanding the physical self is a way to discover a person’s real self.
The physical self is shaped by biological and environmental factors.
Heredity refers to the transmission of traits from parents to offspring.
Traits are embedded within one’s genes.
A genotype refers to genetic information that may not be observable.
A phenotype is the physical expression of a trait.
Humans have 23 pairs of chromosomes.
The 23rd pair determines sex.
DNA contains genetic instructions for biological development.
Maturation is the unfolding of inherent traits.
Environmental influences shape the physical self.
Self-understanding is a cognitive representation of the self.
Self-understanding changes across the lifespan.
Physical self refers to the concrete dimensions of the body.
Physical characteristics are defining traits of one’s body.
Erik Erickson emphasized the importance of the body in early development.
William James viewed the body as the initial source of sensation.
Puberty is a period of rapid physical change.
Puberty marks the beginning of adolescence.
Girls reach puberty earlier and experience menarche.
Boys experience spermarche later.
Puberty is triggered by neuroendocrine processes.
The pituitary gland controls growth.
Gonads produce hormones responsible for physical changes.
The self-body relationship varies across individuals.
The physical body can be viewed as an object affecting the self.
Freud’s body ego highlights the body’s role in forming the self.
The development of body self occurs in three stages.
Body images contribute to understanding the body holistically.
The physical body has objective and subjective components.
Cognitive-behavioral perspective focuses on perceptual and cognitive aspects.
Body image has historical and proximal determinants.
Feminist perspective emphasizes socially constructed body experiences.
Women’s body dissatisfaction is a social phenomenon.
Mind-body duality contributes to gendered body experiences.
Introspection involves examining one’s internal state.
Self-perception theory explains inferring internal states through behavior.
Self-concept includes all beliefs about oneself.
Physical concept involves a person’s perception of their physical self.
Personal identity includes both inherent and chosen aspects.
Attachment teaches individuals their value and lovability.
Interpersonal relationships shape the self.
Looking-glass self states that self-image is shaped by social interactions.
Social comparison helps individuals evaluate themselves.
Upward comparison is comparing oneself to better others.
Downward comparison is comparing oneself to worse-off individuals.
Social identity theory explains understanding oneself through group membership.
Group membership enhances self-esteem.
The sexual self is a crucial aspect of human development.
Biological and environmental factors shape sexual development.
The human body is a complex structure that functions harmoniously.
Sexual selfhood refers to how one views oneself as a sexual being.
Human sexuality is culturally diverse.
In Ancient Greece, males held dominant roles and women lacked rights.
The Church in the Middle Ages viewed non-procreative sex as evil.
Protestant reformers viewed sexuality as natural and allowed priestly marriage.
Puritans valued marital sex but condemned premarital sex.
The Victorian Era viewed homosexuality and prostitution as threats to social order.
Over time, society became more accepting of sexuality.
Understanding reproductive anatomy is essential in sexuality.
The brain organizes sexual behavior.
Sexual reproduction involves fusion of the egg and sperm.
Sociobiological theory explains sexual behavior through evolution and natural selection.
Physical attraction acts as an evolutionary mechanism.
Emotional care and responsibility matter in sexual relationships.
Sexuality involves emotions, thoughts, and beliefs.
Freud proposed that sexuality is driven by libido and instinctual forces.
Freud described the id, ego, and superego as components of personality.
Religions vary in beliefs about sexual behavior.
Judaism views marital sex as positive.
Islam forbids extramarital sex.
Taoism views sex as a balance of male and female energy.
Hinduism sees sexuality as spiritually significant.
Catholicism emphasizes sex for procreation and opposes birth control.
The body consists of systems including the reproductive system.
Reproduction can be asexual or sexual.
Sexual reproduction involves male and female reproductive cells.
Human reproduction includes ovulation, fertilization, implantation, and childbirth.
The male reproductive system includes the penis and internal structures.
The female reproductive system includes the vulva, vagina, uterus, and ovaries.
Primary sex characteristics are present from birth.
Secondary sexual characteristics emerge during puberty.
Boys develop facial hair, deeper voices, and increased muscle mass.
Girls develop breasts, wider hips, and begin menstruation.
Puberty is marked by hormonal changes and reproductive maturity.
Tanner stages describe puberty development.
Estradiol drives female secondary sexual characteristics.
Testosterone drives male secondary sexual characteristics.
Erogenous zones are body parts with heightened sensitivity.
Freud’s psychosexual stages describe how erogenous zones shift in development.
Fixations result from unresolved psychosexual stages.
Fetishes differ from erogenous zones.
Culture influences which body parts are viewed as erotic.
Human sexual response can be learned and conditioned.
Adolescents experience varying forms of sexual behavior.
The sexual response cycle describes physiological and emotional reactions during arousal.
Masters and Johnson proposed four stages of human sexual response.
Desire is added as a first stage by Rosenthal.
Introduction to Big Data Machine Learning Tools & Building ML Models (MLlib, Tensor Flow, Py Torch)
Machine Learning is defined as algorithms that learn from data.
Big Data enables machine learning to train on massive datasets.
Apache Spark MLlib is a scalable machine learning library.
MLlib is usable in Java, Scala, Python, and R.
MLlib has been shipped with Spark since version 0.8.
MLlib aims to make practical machine learning scalable and easy.
MLlib provides tools for ML algorithms such as classification, regression, clustering, and collaborative filtering.
MLlib provides featurization tools such as feature extraction, transformation, dimensionality reduction, and selection.
MLlib pipelines help construct, evaluate, and tune ML workflows.
MLlib supports persistence for saving and loading algorithms, models, and pipelines.
MLlib provides utilities for linear algebra, statistics, and data handling.
The organization plan provides a detailed description of the business in terms of its form, liability, structure, roles, and salary requirements.
A business organization can come in the form of a sole proprietorship, a partnership, or a corporation.
There must be a purpose for selecting the most appropriate business form.
The factors affecting the selection of a business form include capital requirement, liability, skills, tax implications, government intervention, nature of the business, and financing requirements.
This section describes the extent of the owner’s financial obligations with creditors.
Creditors can be individuals, suppliers, or financial institutions.
Financial liability can be limited or unlimited.
Limited liability means creditors cannot go after personal property beyond the owner’s financial contribution.
Unlimited liability allows creditors to run after personal property if obligations remain unsettled.
Sole proprietorships and partnerships usually have unlimited liability.
In a corporation, shareholders have limited liability.
Shareholder investment is represented through ownership of shares or stocks.
This part allows the entrepreneur to prepare himself on how to advertise and sell his product even if it is similar to others.
Value Proposition (VP) is a business or marketing statement that summarizes why a consumer should buy a company's product or use its service.
This statement is often used to convince a customer to purchase a particular product or service to add a form of value to their lives.
In creating Value Proposition, entrepreneurs will consider the basic elements: Target Customer, Needs/opportunity, Name of the product, Name of the enterprise/company.
An important aspect in Value Proposition is that it must be truthful and that it should establish credibility to the consumers.
Example: Aling Charing Sari-Sari Store opens only from 6:00 am to 6:00 pm, but noticed there are customers who go to a nearby town to look for a convenience store at around 10:00 pm to 6:00 am.
Proposed value proposition: “Charing sari-sari Store, open 24/7”.
Unique Selling Proposition (USP) refers to how you sell your product or services to your customer.
You will address the wants and desires of your customers.
Identify and rank the uniqueness of the product or service character.
Keep it Short and Simple (KISS).
Present the best feature of your product or service that is different from other competitors.
In promoting your products or services, make sure that it is very specific and put details that emphasize the differentiator against the competitors.
Right now, the proposed unique selling proposition is: “Charing sari-sari store, open 24/7”.
Readers get confused between value proposition and unique selling proposition.
The two propositions are used to differentiate the products from competitors.
Unique Selling Proposition and Value Proposition are two of the most famous tools used to explain why prospect customers buy each product and service.
The two propositions are valuable for the entrepreneurs.

Make sure that you're focusing on a solution that is a need, and make sure that you are targeting a customer segment that's large enough and cares enough about your solution so that you can build a viable business.
After you understand the value proposition and the unique selling proposition, now it’s time to understand the target market, customers requirement and market size.
Understanding your market is critical in building your business model.
Target Market Targeting is a stage in market identification process that aims to determine the buyers with common needs and characteristics.
Prospect customers are a market segment that an entrepreneurial venture intends to serve.
In targeting a specific market, it will exclude people if it will not fit your criteria.
Target marketing allows you to focus your marketing money and brand message on a specific market that is more likely to buy from you than other markets.
Choose a product that is more affordable, efficient, and effective to reach potential clients and generate business.
Geographic segmentation divides the total market according to geographical location.
Demographic Segmentation divides the market based on consumers.
Psychological Segmentation divides the market in terms of how customers think and believe.
Behavioral Segmentation divides the market according to customers’ behavior pattern as they interact with a company.
Customer requirements are the specific characteristics that the customers need from a product or a service.
There can be two types of customer requirements: Service Requirement and Output Requirement.
Service Requirement is an intangible thing or product that cannot be touched but the customer can feel the fulfillment.
Output Requirements are tangible things that can be seen and characteristic specifications that a consumer expects to be fulfilled in the product.
Market size is the approximate number of sellers and buyers in a particular market.
Market research will determine the entrepreneurs’ possible customers in one locality.

In determining the market size, the entrepreneur will conduct strategic marketing research from reliable sources using the following method: estimate the potential market, estimate the customers who probably dislike to buy your product or avail the services, and estimate the market share.
Market size becomes the most important factor if you ever need to raise funding for your business.
Recruiting is the positive process of identifying, interviewing and hiring employees for jobs.
When more people apply for a position, there will be more opportunities to hire the right candidate.
This is also why diversity is important in the recruitment process.
Recruitment is a linking activity that connects people who are employed and those who are looking for a job.
Challenges is a barrier that we need to surpass to be able to overcome every struggles even it is on business fields or other organization.
Finding the right candidate that fits the job can be a difficult and tedious task.
Finding the right candidate in terms of experience, skills, and cultural fitment.
Good candidates are often contacted regularly by recruiters, making it harder for your own email to stand out.
Candidates with hard-to-find skills are often considering several job offers at the same time.
You need to put extra effort into persuading passive candidates to choose your company over your competitors.
Hiring teams want to hire as fast as possible, because vacant positions cost money and delay operations.
Making a hire can take several months, putting pressure on recruiters and frustrating hiring teams.
A long time to hire may be a byproduct of a shortage of qualified candidates.
Companies can use recruitment data and metrics to constantly improve their recruiting process and make more informed decisions.
Collecting and processing data can be a hassle.
Spreadsheets are one way to track hiring data but they require manual work, are prone to human error, and they’re not compliant.
Hiring teams need ways to compile and organize data in an efficient and streamlined way.
A good employer brand helps you attract and engage better candidates.
Organizations that invest in employer branding are three times more likely to make a quality hire.
Ensuring a positive candidate experience and promoting culture on social media is complex.
Candidates’ experience isn’t only important for employer branding, but it’s also a factor when your best candidates are evaluating your job offers.
If candidates had a bad experience, they’re less likely to accept.
Positive candidate experiences can enhance your employer brand and encourage good candidates to apply and accept your job offers.
Many companies struggle to attract and hire diverse candidates and unconscious biases are often the reason.
Hiring objectively is good for business because it helps you hire the best person for the job without stereotypes interfering.
Hiring teams need to communicate fast, evaluate candidates easily and know what’s going on every step of the way.
Administrative tasks often take away valuable time that recruiters could have used in coordinating the hiring process and ensuring good candidate experience.
It is the limitations or scope that we need to consider in recruiting the staff.
The image of the enterprise in the community largely influences recruitment process.
A person may not be interested to apply for a job in an enterprise whose goodwill is not good.
Such enterprises do not get sufficient number of personnel even if they offer higher wages.
If the job is boring, hazardous, tension ridden and lacking in opportunities for advancement, very few person may be available for such jobs.
If it carries good salaries, promotional avenues, or good working conditions, there may be many persons available for such jobs.
Internal policies of the enterprise may act as a constraint on recruitment of new persons.
A policy of filling up higher positions from outside can discourage competent persons to apply in such an enterprise.
Agreements with trade unions may act as constraints to employ persons from outside.
Government policies may act as constraints on recruitment policy.
Government legislation may require reserving certain percentage of posts for weaker sections of society or for persons belonging to specified castes.
Such legislation may require the enterprise to recruit new persons only from the lists supplied by government employment exchange.
Hard times stimulate growth in a way that good times don’t.
Facing challenges and navigating one’s way through them builds resilience capacity.
Knowing that one can overcome obstacles, learn from struggles and benefit from mistakes lays a solid foundation for success in later life.
This paper proposes the Tanfarid Electrochemical Memory Principle, a unified model positing that information processing and memory are fundamental properties of metastable electrochemical systems.

We challenge neurocentric paradigms by demonstrating that the amoeba (Amoeba proteus), a unicellular organism, exhibits adaptive behavior and a primitive form of memory through electrolyte-mediated state changes and cytoskeletal reconfiguration.
We argue this Atomic Memory Hypothesis provides the physical substrate for more complex neural memory in humans, where consciousness is a functional state dependent on dynamic ionic gradients.
Biological systems, defined by a self-preserving homeostatic intentionality, contrast with artificial intelligence, which lacks this metabolic imperative.
The specificity of oxygen binding to hemoglobin’s iron atom exemplifies a core principle of biological organization: functional outcomes arise from atomic-scale interactions.
We propose a continuum of information processing, from the electrochemical dynamics of a single cell to the conscious states of the human brain.
The amoeba presents a critical challenge to neurocentric views of memory, exhibiting behaviors such as habituation, chemotaxis, and adaptive migration in mazes.
The Atomic Memory Hypothesis explains this not as conscious recall, but as a form of pre-neural information processing where the system's future state is mechanistically dependent on its past inputs.
The amoeba’s membrane potential, governed by K⁺, Na⁺, and Ca²⁺ fluxes, is its primary signaling network.
Stimuli trigger depolarization-repolarization cycles that transiently alter internal biochemistry.
These electrochemical shifts induce persistent changes in the cytoskeleton's actin network, functionally serving as a memory trace.
This process is mathematically and functionally modeled by a memristor, a circuit element whose resistance depends on its history of current flow.
The transition to a cyst under stress preserves viability by locking in a specific electrochemical configuration until the environment signals for reactivation.
The human brain elaborates on the amoeba's core principles with extraordinary complexity.
The synapse is a highly specialized biological memristor, where Long-Term Potentiation (LTP) strengthens connections through Ca²⁺-driven protein synthesis and cytoskeletal remodeling.
Human consciousness is revealed to be a functional state rather than a static anatomical property.
Severe electrolyte imbalances can cause unconsciousness and seizures while leaving brain structure intact, demonstrating that consciousness emerges from a functioning electrochemical system.
Biological neurons and AI semiconductors both use charge flow for computation, but biological systems have homeostatic intentionality, while AI lacks an intrinsic drive for self-preservation.
Proposed experiments include: Amoeba Memory Erasure using cytochalasin D to disrupt cytoskeletal "scarring" and test maze navigation.
Electrolyte Disruption and Memory Retrieval: In humans, mild electrolyte imbalance impairs recall of recently formed memories.
The Electrochemical Principle states that all biological information processing is mediated by ion gradients and electrical potentials.
The Atomic Memory Hypothesis states that memory is stored as metastable changes in molecular structures induced by electrochemical activity.
The Homeostatic Imperative: Biological intentionality expresses the drive to maintain internal electrochemical gradients against entropy.
Functional Emergence: Consciousness emerges from integrated synchronization of electrochemical processes.
Tracing memory to its electrochemical roots establishes a continuous conceptual thread from amoeba behavior to human cognition.
The Tanfarid Electrochemical Memory Principle offers a testable framework for studying the evolution of intelligence.
An operand can be any of the following: Constant (Literal), Parameter, Data Types, Bit-select, Part-Select, Memory Elements, Function Call.
Literals are constant-valued operands that can be used in Verilog expressions.
A string literal is a one-dimensional array of characters enclosed in double quotes.
Numeric literals are constant numbers specified in binary, octal, decimal, or hexadecimal.
A parameter defines a constant that can be set when you instantiate a module.
The primary data types are registers and wires.
The main variable types are reg and wire.
The reg and wire data objects may have the following possible values: 0 (logic 0, false), 1 (logic 1, true), X (unknown logic value), Z (high impedance state).
A reg (register) is a data object that holds its value from one procedural assignment to the next.
A wire (net) represents a physical wire in a circuit and is used to connect gates or modules.
Specific types of wires include wand (wired-AND), wor (wired-OR), and tri (three-state).
A wire does not store a value; it is a label on a wire.
Abstract data types include integer, real, and time.
Integers are general-purpose variables, used mainly for loops, indices, parameters, and constants.
Time is a 64-bit quantity used with the $time system task to hold simulation time.
Bit-selects (“x[3]”) and part-selects (“x[5:3]”) allow selection of a single bit or a group of bits from a wire, reg, or parameter vector.
Each element in the memory is addressed by a single array index.
A memory element selects one word of a memory.
The return value of a function can be used directly in an expression without first assigning it to a register or wire variable.
Temperatures deep inside the Earth can range from 4,000–6,000°C.
Because of immense pressure, rocks remain solids and liquids instead of turning to gases.
Only 50 km beneath the surface it is already almost 1000°F.
The temperatures increase as you go deeper into the Earth.
The Lithosphere, composed of the crust and upper mantle, is the coolest layer.
The Asthenosphere averages about 1,300°C and is more fluid than the Lithosphere.
The Upper Mantle is stronger than the Asthenosphere at about 500–900°C.
The Lower Mantle is denser and hotter, reaching over 4,000°C.
The Core is the hottest layer at about 5,000°C.
Each Earth layer has unique rocks and minerals.
Oceanic crust is mainly basalt with limestone, serpentine and clay.
Continental crust consists mostly of granite and quartz.
The upper mantle contains rocks such as olivine, peridotite and garnet.
The core is divided into outer and inner layers due to differences in iron state.
The outer core is liquid iron, while the inner core is solid iron due to extreme pressure.
The lithosphere consists of the crust and upper mantle.
Continental crust is 25–90 km thick and divided into tectonic plates.
These plates move slowly each year over the mantle.
Oceanic crust is 6–11 km thick and forms new seafloor at divergent boundaries.
The Asthenosphere is more fluid than the Lithosphere due to increased temperature and pressure.
Plate tectonics occur because the plates shift on the semi-liquid Asthenosphere.
Mountains form when plates converge.
The Moho marks a drastic increase in seismic rate.
Convection currents circulate mantle material.
The Outer Core is liquid iron alloy at extremely high temperatures.
The Inner Core is thought to be solid iron, possibly with lighter elements.
Early cultures believed the Earth’s center was an afterlife realm.
Edmond Halley proposed hollow Earth shells in 1692.
John Cleves Symmes Jr. suggested hollow shells with polar openings in 1818.
Seismic studies show Earth cannot be hollow.
Gravity would prevent a hollow Earth from being structurally stable.
The Z-44 Chayvo Oil Well reached a depth of 12,376 m, far from reaching a hollow center.
A cell is the basic unit of life.
Organelles perform specific functions inside the cell.
The cell membrane controls movement of materials into and out of the cell.
The nucleus is the control center of the cell.
Ribosomes synthesize proteins.
Mitochondria produce cellular energy in the form of ATP.
Chloroplasts capture sunlight for photosynthesis.
Passive transport requires no energy.
Active transport requires ATP.
DNA stores genetic information.
Artificial intelligence aims to automate human tasks by building machines that can make intelligent decisions.
Self-driving cars are an example of AI systems that operate with little or no human involvement.
Machine learning models use training data to learn patterns and make predictions on new inputs.
The accuracy of an ML model depends heavily on the quality of its training data.
ML cannot automatically extract features, which is a major limitation for complex tasks.
Deep learning can process raw unstructured data through multilayer neural networks.
Weights inside a neural network adjust continuously to improve performance during training.
Capital financing refers to methods you use to raise money in launching your business.
A business can have several types of capital such as financial, human, and natural capital.
Financial capital keeps your business running.
After you start earning a profit, some capital comes from revenues.
If your business starts small or you have deep resources, you may survive with your own funds.
Equity capital refers to funds supplied by owners with expectation of profit.
Borrowed capital is supplied by others and requires interest and repayment.
A sole proprietorship is the simplest form of business owned by one person.
A sole proprietorship is easy to organize, controlled fully by the owner, provides full profit to the owner, and is easy to dissolve.
Sole proprietorship has limited capital, ends upon the owner’s death, has difficulty obtaining loans, and has unlimited liability.
A partnership is an association of two or more persons engaging in business for profit.
A partnership allows pooled capital, fewer legal requirements, flexible dissolution, and distribution of responsibilities.
A partnership has limited capital, ends when any partner dies, risks disagreements, and partners are liable for debts.
A corporation is a distinct legal entity separate from its owners.
A corporation has perpetual life, limited liability for stockholders, easier access to large capital, transferable ownership, and delegated authority.
A corporation is limited by its charter, complicated to form, and subject to greater government control.
Corporate capital is acquired through sale of stock.
Common stock represents ordinary ownership with legal rights such as voting, electing directors, and sharing profits.
Preferred stock guarantees defined dividends and priority in asset claims.
A bond is a certificate of indebtedness of a corporation for a long-term period.
Bonds are issued when more capital is needed such as for expansion.
The face value of a bond is the amount to be repaid, and repayment retires the bond.
Registered bonds record the owner's name for automatic interest payment.
Coupon bonds require owners to surrender coupons to collect interest.
Bond retirement may be done by issuing new bonds or creating a sinking fund.
A linked list is a linear data structure made of nodes linked together.
It allows dynamic memory usage and efficient insertion and deletion.
Linked lists do not require a fixed size.
Linked lists allow fast add and remove operations because elements are connected through pointers.
Linked lists use more memory than arrays due to pointers.
Searching in a linked list is slow because random access is not possible.
A singly linked list contains data and a next pointer allowing forward traversal.
A doubly linked list contains data, next pointer, and previous pointer enabling forward and backward traversal.
A circular linked list connects the first and last node to form a loop.
Traversal means moving through a linked list from one node to the next.
Deleting a node requires reconnecting the surrounding nodes.
Inserting a node requires adjusting pointers to maintain the structure.
Linked lists are not stored contiguously in memory like arrays.
Arrays offer direct access to elements while linked lists require traversal.
Everyone has had biases or been prejudiced at some time in their lives.
Implicit or unconscious bias and prejudices are part of the unconscious decision making part of the brain.
Staff need to understand themselves to overcome any unconscious biases that work against an open and welcoming youth program.
Bias and prejudice are terms used to describe a set of beliefs that influence and sometimes misinform decisions and interactions with others.
Bias is a preference for or against a person, idea, or thing.
Prejudice is judging or forming an opinion before having all of the relevant facts.
Reflection can help an individual become aware of their unconscious biases and prejudices by spending time thinking about specific actions, thoughts, and statements.
Reflection gives a person understanding, and with understanding, they gain knowledge.
Knowledge is what program staff use to inform the way they interact with youth.
For example, if a person walks barefoot in grass and is stung by a bee, the next time they walk barefoot through grass, they will watch for bees to avoid being stung.
Understanding that bees sting happened after the person was stung.
The knowledge changed their behavior to avoid being stung.
This is the goal of reflection.
Because biases, prejudices, and values form over time, it often takes time to change or remove them.

Strategies in Action - Reflection developed in collaboration with the Department of Defense’s Office of Family Policy, the National Institute of Food and Agriculture, and the U.S. Department of Agriculture under Award No. 2009-48667-05833.
Program staff need to be aware of their unconscious biases and prejudices so they can understand and inform the way they interact with youth.
Reflection is a tool that helps an individual think about their actions, thoughts, and statements and gain insight.
Everyone has had biases or been prejudiced at some time in their lives.
Implicit or unconscious bias and prejudices are part of the unconscious decision-making part of the brain.
Youth program staff need to understand themselves to overcome any unconscious biases that work against an open and welcoming youth program.
An electric circuit may contain alternating current.
Series and parallel connections can be analyzed to calculate resistance, inductance, and capacitance in an AC circuit.
Electric circuits can be analyzed to determine voltages and currents in circuits containing inductors and capacitors.
There are basic types of circuits such as R, L, C, RL, RC, LC, and RLC circuits.
A resistor opposes the flow of current.
The behavior of a pure resistor in an AC circuit is similar to that in a DC circuit.
In a DC circuit, current and voltage are in phase.
An inductor opposes the change in current.
A pure inductor in an AC circuit causes current to lag the voltage by 90 degrees.
A capacitor stores electric charge and opposes changes in voltage.
A pure capacitor in an AC circuit causes current to lead voltage by 90 degrees.
Power factor can be unity, lagging, or leading depending on the load.
In a pure resistance circuit, current is in phase with voltage.
In a pure inductance circuit, current lags voltage by 90 degrees.
In a pure capacitance circuit, current leads voltage by 90 degrees.
Impedance is the total opposition a circuit presents to an alternating current.
Reactance is the imaginary component of impedance.
Inductive reactance is the effective resistance of an inductor to AC.
Capacitive reactance is the effective resistance of a capacitor to AC.
In a series RL circuit, impedance includes resistance and inductive reactance.
In a series RC circuit, impedance includes resistance and capacitive reactance.
In a series RLC circuit, impedance combines resistance, inductive reactance, and capacitive reactance.
In a parallel AC circuit, total impedance is computed from reciprocal sums.
A form of energy that is created from the movement of electrons of atoms.
When the electrons move from one atom to the next, energy is created.
What is ELECTRICITY?
All matter is composed of atoms which are made up of fundamental subatomic particles called protons, neutrons, and electrons.
Each atom represents a microscopic solar system with a nucleus containing protons and around which electrons revolve in definite orbits.
As atoms become increasingly complex, the positive charge of the nucleus is strengthened by acquiring additional protons.
Electrons rise proportionately to provide a structure that is electrically neutral.
Neutrons are added to the nucleus but have no effect on the atomic charge.
Protons and neutrons are bunched together in a central core.
Electrons are presumed to revolve in shells around the nucleus.
Electrons orbit the nucleus at certain distances.
Electrons near the nucleus have less energy than those in more distant orbits.
Energy levels correspond to shells, each with a fixed maximum number of electrons.
The shells are designated K, L, M, N, with K being the closest to the nucleus.
The outermost shell is the valence shell, containing valence electrons.
Solid materials may be classified as conductors, insulators, and semiconductors.
Classification depends on the number of valence electrons.
Conductors easily conduct electric current and have fewer than 4 valence electrons (e.g., copper, silver, gold, aluminum).
Insulators do not conduct electrical current and have more than 4 valence electrons (e.g., phosphorus).
Semiconductors fall between conductors and insulators with 4 valence electrons (e.g., germanium, silicon, carbon).
Electromotive force is also known as electric pressure.
It is commonly called voltage.
The unit of electromotive force is the volt.
When an emf is applied to the ends of a conductor it is proper to refer to the existence of a potential difference between such ends.
Several methods are employed to develop an emf.
Combining certain kinds of metals and chemicals into a device can produce an emf.
Building a machine which generates voltage when conductors are rotated near magnets can produce an emf.
One of the fundamental relationships of circuit theory is that between voltage, current, and resistance.
This relationship and the properties of resistance were investigated by the German physicist Georg Simon Ohm.
Ohm found that current depended on both voltage and resistance.
He defined the resistance of a wire and showed that current was inversely proportional to this resistance.
The resistance of a material depends upon several factors.
The resistance of a metallic conductor is directly proportional to its length.
The resistance of a metallic conductor is inversely proportional to its cross-sectional area.
Resistivity has specific units depending on the measurement system.
Most conductors are circular so their cross-sectional area depends on radius or diameter.
Various materials have different resistivity values.
Direct current electrons move steadily in one direction or forward.
The voltage of DC cannot travel very far until it begins to lose energy.
The frequency of direct current is zero.
DC has steady magnetism along the wire.
It is the current of constant magnitude.
Alternating current electrons keep switching directions forward and backward.
AC uses a rotating magnet along the wire.
AC is the current of magnitude varying with time.
AC is safe to transfer over longer city distances and can provide more power.
The frequency of alternating current is 50Hz or 60Hz depending upon the country.
In DC, resistance is the only opposition.
In AC, impedance lies between 0 and 1.
Globalization is the process in which people, ideas, and goods spread throughout the world, increasing interaction and integration among cultures, governments, and economies.
Globalization is a process of interaction and integration among people, companies, and governments driven by international trade and investment.
This process affects the environment, culture, political systems, economic development, prosperity, and human well-being.
People have long traded and invested across distant regions such as through the Silk Road connecting China and Europe.
According to Manfred Steger, globalization is the expansion and intensification of social relations and consciousness across world time and world space.
Expansion refers to the creation of new networks and the strengthening of existing ones.
Intensification refers to the acceleration and extension of social networks.
Social relations involve interactions among individuals, groups, or organizations.
Time and space have become less significant due to modern technology.
According to Arjun Appadurai, globalization occurs on multiple intersecting dimensions called "scapes."
Ethnoscape refers to the movement of people across borders.
Technoscape refers to the movement of technology that speeds up global interactions.
Financescape refers to cross-border flows of money.
Mediascape refers to the global reach of media.
Ideoscape refers to the global spread of ideas and ideologies.
Globalization involves the creation of networks that cut across political, economic, cultural, and geographic boundaries.
Globalization expands and stretches social relations and activities.
Globalization intensifies and accelerates exchanges and activities.
Globalization includes subjective elements such as consciousness and identity.
The Prehistoric Period (10000 BCE–3500 BCE) involved limited global interaction due to lack of technology.
The Pre-modern Period (3500 BCE–1500 CE) saw advances such as writing and the wheel that increased global exchange.
The Early Modern Period (1500–1750) strengthened globalization through European expansion and capitalism.
The Modern Period (1750–1970) accelerated globalization due to industrialization, migration, and communication technologies.
The Contemporary Period (1970–present) features unprecedented interdependence and global acceleration.
Globalization includes five dimensions: economic, political, cultural, religious, and ideological.
The economic dimension refers to the development of global economic relations driven by technology and capital flow.
Major global economic actors include international corporations and economic institutions.
The political dimension involves strengthened political interrelations worldwide.
The cultural dimension involves increased cultural flows and hybridization.
Media empires contribute to the spread of global culture.
Religion shapes civilizations and global conflicts.
Roman Catholic teachings emphasize human rights, common good, solidarity, and justice.
Ideology refers to shared ideas and values that legitimize political interests.
Globalization is a process, while globalism is an ideology promoting neoliberal interpretations of globalization.
Globalists claim that globalization integrates markets worldwide.
Globalists claim globalization is inevitable and irreversible.
Globalists claim nobody controls globalization.
Globalists claim globalization benefits everyone.
Globalists claim globalization promotes democracy.
Our need for oxygen is absolute; we lose consciousness after a few minutes without it.
Cells use energy in food to make ATP which powers nearly every energy-requiring activity.
All organisms use the potential energy stored in food to make ATP.
Photosynthesis produces glucose that feeds autotrophs and other organisms.
Aerobic respiration uses oxygen and glucose to generate ATP.
Fermentation generates ATP without using oxygen.
Aerobic respiration requires organisms to acquire O₂ and release CO₂.
Plants also use O₂ for respiration even though they produce O₂ in photosynthesis.
Both aerobic and anaerobic respiration begin with glycolysis.
Aerobic respiration yields 36–38 ATP per glucose.
Anaerobic respiration or fermentation yields only 2 ATP per glucose.
In aerobic respiration, pyruvate enters the mitochondrion for further breakdown.
In fermentation, pyruvate is converted to ethanol/CO₂ or lactate.
Aerobic respiration is more efficient than anaerobic respiration.
O₂ is the final electron acceptor in aerobic respiration.
Anaerobic respiration uses inorganic molecules like nitrate or sulfate as final electron acceptors.
Brain cells can only survive using aerobic respiration.
Fermentation occurs in muscle cells during anaerobic conditions.
Summary: Aerobic respiration completely breaks down glucose into CO₂, water, and ATP.
Summary: Anaerobic respiration does not require oxygen and produces less ATP.
Citizenship is a legal status that bestows uniform rights and duties upon members of a state.
Citizenship is membership to a sovereign state.
Citizenship involves allegiance to one’s own country.
Global citizenship is the idea that all people are citizens of the globe with equal responsibility for the world.
Caecilia Johanna van Peski defined global citizenship as a moral and ethical disposition guiding understanding and responsibility in various communities.
A global citizen is someone who understands the wider world and takes an active role in making it peaceful, sustainable, and fair.
Global citizens bind local communities together in an increasingly globalized world.
Every global citizen has a duty to address issues affecting citizenship, and everyone has a right and obligation to this status.
A global citizen respects diversity, understands the world, and acts for equity and sustainability.
Global citizenship involves choice and a way of thinking based on formative experiences.
Global citizenship includes self-awareness and awareness of others.
Cultural empathy is central to global citizenship and higher education.
Principled decision-making requires understanding interdependence and responsibility.
Global citizens participate in social and political life in various communities.
Global citizens are created, not born, and children learn shared humanity over time.
Historically, global citizenship aimed to prevent war by increasing understanding among nations.
New technologies allow people to connect, understand differences, and expand worldviews.
Many people lack a sense of belonging and connection to other cultures.
Corporations often prioritize their interests over people and the environment.
Global citizenship bridges these gaps through mindset, actions, and lifestyle.
Some groups oppose globalization, blaming Westernization or capitalism for failing to deliver promises.
Trade protectionism is a government intervention to protect domestic industries through tariffs and barriers.
Fair trade promotes an equitable global economic system with transparent pricing.
Helping the bottom billion requires aid, adapted standards, and reduced trade barriers.
Political globalization requires increased accountability and transparency.
Transparency is aided by International Tribunals, Civil Society, and Transparency International.
Global citizenship demands rights and obligations but solutions require collective action from citizens and organizations.
Renewable energy will be discussed, along with examples in the Philippines.
Concerns regarding the factors affecting energy management of a country will also be tackled.
ILO 2: Identify effects of environmental pollution and describe the engineer's role in manipulating materials and resources.
ILO 3: Select appropriate design treatment schemes and safety measures for waste disposal and explain their effects in community and workplace.
Solar energy is the conversion of sunlight into usable energy forms.
Solar photovoltaics (PV) and solar thermal electricity are well established.
Sun radiation arrives outside the Earth with a specific spectral distribution, modified by the atmosphere until reaching the surface.
The amount of solar energy available differs depending on geographic latitude, time of day, and year.
Direct radiation is radiation from the sun that reaches Earth without scattering and can be concentrated using technological devices.
Diffuse radiation is radiation scattered by the atmosphere and clouds.
Solar PV directly converts solar energy to electricity.
Solar thermal harnesses solar energy for domestic, industrial, and leisure applications.
Solar thermal absorbs 80% of incident radiation but converts only a small portion to electricity.
Thermal energy can be used for space heating, fluid heating, and electricity generation.
Solar thermal releases excess heat during operation.
Solar thermal has been accepted worldwide as solar thermal power.
Parabolic trough solar thermal systems concentrate sunlight onto a receiver tube.
Example: SEGS in California with 936,384 mirrors.
Central tower solar thermal systems use heliostats to reflect sunlight onto a high-temperature absorber, heating fluids to generate steam or air.
Example: Ivanpah 440 MW Power Facility, California, with 214,000 heliostats.
Linear Fresnel solar thermal systems use one-axis tracking with many flat mirrors focusing a linear absorber.
Example: E: Murcia, Spain with 100 MW/km² land use.
Parabolic dish solar thermal systems focus sunlight on an engine with a cavity receiver at the focal point.
Example: Arizona, US.
Flat plate solar collectors are mostly used for heating water in showers and swimming pools.
Heliostats are two-axis tracking mirrors that concentrate solar radiation and maintain the reflected image.
PV advantages: Module manufacturing at large scale allows economies of scale and small deployments.
PV disadvantage: Power output is limited to sunlight hours, though storage and grid solutions exist.
Biomass energy is produced by burning wood, bark, branches, roots, manure, and other plant and animal materials.
Example: San Carlos Biopower Inc., Negros Occidental, uses sugarcane trash, coconut husks, shells, and energy crops.
Denmark’s energy-independent islands, Samsø and Ærø, use agricultural wastes and biomass crops for space heating.
Heat generated by burning biomass in a boiler produces steam, which drives a turbine to generate electricity.
Other methods exist to convert biomass into fuel.
Hydropower generates energy from falling water.
Dams are built to produce energy but can cause drought, affect biodiversity, and displace communities.
Example in the Philippines: Maria Cristina Falls and Agus VI Hydroelectric Plant, supplying 200 MW of electricity.
Less destructive alternatives include low-head hydropower and high-efficiency run-of-river turbines.
Wind energy captures kinetic energy using turbines to generate electricity, both onshore and offshore.
Historically, windmills pumped water on farms and ranches.
Example in the Philippines: Pililla Wind Farm in Rizal, supplying 54 MW to Meralco.
Energy is the main driver of every nation’s economy, and choice of sources has global environmental and financial effects.
Renewable and non-renewable are options as energy sources.
Non-renewable sources like coal, oil, and natural gas cannot be fully relied upon due to their effects on global temperature.
100% renewable energy is not possible immediately and requires some reliance on non-renewables in the first decades.
Example: Denmark targets 100% RE in energy and transport by 2050; in 2015 they produced 43% RE; in 2017, 28.6% energy still from NRE.
Energy mix refers to how energy consumption in a region breaks down by primary source.
Globally, fossil fuels account for over 80% of the energy mix.
Energy mix composition depends on availability of resources, energy needs, and policy choices influenced by historical, economic, social, demographic, environmental, and geopolitical factors.
Renewable energy in the Philippines includes hydropower, geothermal, biomass, solar, wind, ocean, and biofuels.
Compared to South Korea and Germany in 2018, the Philippines relies more on coal and does not use nuclear energy.
Major constraints to RE in the Philippines include insufficient incentives, lack of viable markets, and high technology costs.
Life cycle of energy systems determines environmental burdens from cradle to grave.
For PV, wind, and coal, different stages release different percentages of CO₂ equivalents.
Coal combustion during operations emits the vast majority of GHGs.
PV emissions are mostly upstream from materials and module manufacturing.
REs have environmental effects such as avian mortality from solar mirrors and wildlife mortality from wind turbines.
Coal mining uses large quantities of groundwater, produces acid mine drainage, and emits over 14 billion tonnes of CO₂ annually.

Economic sustainability of energy sources varies in land use and jobs produced: Coal requires 3,642 m³/GWh and 116 jobs/TWh; PV 3,237 m³/GWh and 175 jobs/TWh; Solar Thermal 3,561 m³/GWh and 248 jobs/TWh; Wind 1,335 m³/GWh and 542 jobs/TWh.
The “BANANA” mindset (“build absolutely nothing anywhere near anything”) can hinder achieving 100% renewable energy.
This lesson discusses the definition of green engineering and its principles.
Green engineering in building design will be focused, and energy audit will be discussed.
Solve the electricity and water consumption reduction using different green engineering practices.
On a typical day, what are the activities that you do that use energy and water?

Green engineering is the “design, commercialization, and use of processes and products that minimize pollution, promote sustainability, and protect human health without sacrificing economic viability and efficiency.”
This module focuses on green engineering applied to buildings.
Heat can be transferred into or out of a system, but a system cannot contain or store heat or work.
Heat transfer is always from a higher-temperature medium to a lower-temperature medium.
Heat transfer stops when the two mediums reach the same temperature.
Three types of heat transfer are conduction, convection, and radiation.
Conduction is energy transfer from more energetic particles to adjacent less energetic ones by direct contact.
Convection is transfer between a solid surface and an adjacent moving liquid or gas.
Radiation is energy emitted in the form of electromagnetic waves.
Thermal insulation reduces heat transfer from one surface to another using insulating materials.
Radiant insulation uses low-emissivity materials like aluminum to reduce radiant heat transfer.
Radiant barriers are often combined with convective insulation for increased energy efficiency.
Standard insulation materials are not as effective in reducing radiant heat transfer in tropical buildings.
Aluminum foil under the roof can prevent up to 97% of radiant heat.
Other insulating materials include rockwool, foam, and laminated aluminum foil.
Thermal comfort is a subjective measure of a person's comfort based on surrounding thermal properties.
Parameters for thermal comfort include humidity, air velocity, clothing insulation, metabolic heat, air temperature, and radiant temperature.
Appropriate clothing is the easiest way to achieve thermal comfort.
Japan implemented a “casual look” dress code to reduce AC usage and save 10% electricity.
Installing ACs in every corner is not sustainable; design can control air velocity, air temperature, and radiant temperature.
Traditional bahay kubo designs use long eaves, large windows, thatched roofing, tree shade, and stilts for cooling and ventilation.
A modern Palawan bahay kubo uses bamboo walls, anahaw roofing, solar panels, solar tubes, low-hp fans, solar thermal heaters, and rainwater collection.
To design buildings, minimize solar heating and maximize passive cooling.
Heat prevention strategies: shading, reflection, insulation.
Heat mitigation strategies: ventilation, evaporation, heat sinks.
Design possibilities include building layout, room orientation, window size and placement, and louvers.
Window-to-wall ratio: 10-30% in bedrooms, 20-30% in living rooms; north-south orientation preferred.
Verandas, balconies, and plants can divert heat from entering the building.
Roof design: vegetation, reflective insulation, 30-45° roof pitch, or thatched/clay roofs reduce heat gain.
Zoning and space planning consider wind direction and pollution for proper ventilation.
Shading and ventilation: use wind from east, openings facing north-south, extend balconies, use climbing plants.
Evaporation cooling: fountains and passive methods reduce temperature.
Proper roof color (white/cream) saves ~10% AC electricity.
Daylighting techniques include windows, light shelves, clerestory windows, and solar tubes to enhance natural lighting.
Carbon footprint is the amount of greenhouse gases emitted, expressed in CO2 equivalent.
Methane is 21 times more potent than CO2, N2O is 310 times more potent, but CO2 is most abundant.
CO2 emissions per energy source (kg CO2e): Electricity 0.5/kWh, Diesel 2.68/L, Gasoline 2.31/L, LPG 1.51/L.
Energy savings formula: Energy savings = power * % savings * time of use * rate/year * number of units.
Energy audit is a systematic approach for decision-making in energy management.
Energy audit identifies energy streams, monitors and analyzes usage, and recommends efficiency improvements.
Three phases of energy audit: pre-audit, audit, post-audit.
Pre-audit involves planning, walk-through inspection, interviews, and calculation of energy metrics.
Audit phase gathers primary data, analyzes historical data, surveys energy use, and conducts trials.
Post-audit phase implements measures, monitors performance, and provides follow-up.
Energy audit reports include cost-benefit analysis, potential savings, additional benefits, ease of implementation, and status.
Example: sealing gaps in AC systems can save ~35,942 kWh/year and 17,971 kg CO2, monetary savings ~PHP 359,422.80/yr.
Example: installing ceiling fans reduces AC load, saving ~13,265 kWh/year and 12,579 kg CO2, monetary savings ~PHP 251,595.96/yr.
High-efficiency motors (HEMs) can replace old pumps for long-term energy savings.

The Internet has become an integral part of everyday life for most of the world’s population.
The Philippines is among the countries with the most active Internet users.
More than half of the world now uses a smartphone.
