More than half of the world’s web traffic now comes from mobile phones.
More than half of all mobile connections around the world are now “broadband.”
More than one in five of the world’s population shopped online in the past 30 days.
Self-presentation is the process of controlling how one is perceived by other people.
Individuals selectively provide information to construct positive images.
Anything posted online should be considered “public,” regardless of privacy settings.
Personal identity differentiates the individual as unique from others.
Social identity identifies the individual by group memberships.
Digital devices help us share information broadly, more than ever before.
Diaries that were once private are now posted as blogs for public viewing.
Arm’s-length self-photography, like selfies, represents a major change in self-presentation.
People sometimes forget the extent of information they share online.
Sharing online adds a sense of confidence, especially when others like or share posts.
The “disinhibition effect” allows people to express themselves more freely online.
Lack of face-to-face interaction and anonymity gives freedom for self-disclosure but can also cause conflict.
Some individuals express their "true self" better online than face-to-face.
Anonymity can help shy or introverted individuals express thoughts without threat to self-esteem.
Relationships may be strengthened or broken through posts online.
We should verify online information before believing or promoting it.
We should think carefully before posting to prevent conflict or cyberbullying.
Sex corresponds to biological state (male/female) but is socially constructed.
Gender is the social understanding of how sex is experienced and manifested.
Sexuality is an individual expression of desire and is often fluid.
Smart sharing guidelines help protect oneself and others online.
Consider necessity, benefit, appropriateness, and family resolution before posting.
Avoid sharing emotional or unresolved personal issues publicly.
Follow proper Internet ethics: safer sites, guard passwords, limit sharing, be responsible online.
Anything posted online stays online, even if deleted.
Do not embarrass or harm others online.
Report strange or bad behavior online.
Be choosy about online friends and be patient.
Metacognition is commonly defined as “thinking about thinking”.
It is the awareness of the scope and limitations of your current knowledge and skills.
Metacognition enables the person to adapt existing knowledge and skills to approach a learning task for optimum results.
Metacognition has two aspects: self-appraisal and self-management.
Self-appraisal is personal reflection on one’s knowledge and capabilities.
Self-management is the mental process of using existing skills to plan and adapt to successfully learn or accomplish a task.
Elements of Metacognition: metacognitive knowledge and metacognition regulation.
Metacognitive knowledge is the learner’s awareness of one’s ability to understand and learn concepts and knowledge about learning strategies.
Metacognition regulation is the learner’s ability to observe, plan, evaluate, and adapt learning strategies.
Metacognitive knowledge has three types: declarative, procedural, and strategy knowledge.
Declarative knowledge is understanding one’s capacities and subjective assessment.
Procedural knowledge is awareness of one’s knowledge and assessment of task difficulty.
Strategy knowledge is the ability to use and adapt strategies to acquire information, learn skills, and understand concepts.
Skills to exercise metacognition include knowing your limits, modifying your approach, skimming, rehearsing, and self-testing.
Knowing your limits means understanding the scope and limitations of your resources.
Modifying your approach begins with recognizing when a strategy is inappropriate or comprehension is lacking.
Skimming involves browsing material and focusing on keywords, phrases, or sentences.
Rehearsing is repeatedly practicing while making personal interpretations or summaries.
Self-test involves checking comprehension and skills acquired during learning.
Self-regulated learners are conscious of their strengths and weaknesses and actively improve their learning.
The Self-Regulated Learning model, by Barry Zimmerman, has three phases: forethought, performance control, and self-reflection.
Forethought is preparing and expecting the learning task.
Performance control is organizing and performing procedures while learning, including self-talk and self-monitoring.
Self-reflection is reflecting at the end of a task and matching results to objectives.
Engaged students take greater responsibility for learning and improve academic performance.
The SRL model follows a sequence of planning, practice, and evaluation.
A goal is the desired outcome anticipated by a person or organization.
Goals may be long-term or short-term and achieved through planning, execution, and evaluation.
Goal setting establishes a standard to guide actions.
Goals help concentrate energy, arrange strategies, create a purpose-centered life, and provide a sense of accomplishment.
Locke (1969) said working toward a goal is a major source of motivation and improves performance.
90% of studies show specific and challenging goals lead to higher performance.
Goals influence performance by directing attention, mobilizing effort, increasing determination, and encouraging plan development.
Burton (1983) claimed effective goal-setting reduces stress, improves focus, confidence, and satisfaction.
Goal-setting theory refers to the effects of setting goals on subsequent performance.
Edwin Locke found specific, difficult goals lead to better performance than general, easy goals.
Locke (1996) based goal setting on Aristotle’s final causality: action caused by purpose.
Locke proposed five principles of goal-setting: clarity, challenge, commitment, feedback, and task complexity.
Clarity: clear, measurable goals are more achievable.
Challenge: goals must be difficult enough to motivate.
Commitment: deliberate effort and accountability increase success.
Feedback: monitor progress toward a goal.
Task complexity: allow enough time to overcome the learning curve for complex goals.
The Five Golden Rules of Goal Setting: set motivating goals, set SMART goals, write goals, make an action plan, stick with it.
Motivating goals must be important and valuable to the individual.
SMART goals: Specific, Measurable, Attainable, Relevant, Time-Bound.
Writing goals makes them real and tangible.
Action plans break goals into steps and track progress.
Stick with it: review goals, set reminders, and maintain relevance, value, and necessity.
Dweck’s Fixed and Growth Mindset Theory describes two types of mindset: fixed and growth.
Fixed mindset: belief that success depends on innate ability; failure is feared.
Growth mindset: belief that success depends on effort, learning, and perseverance; failure is a learning opportunity.
Fixed-mindset individuals want to look smart and avoid looking dumb.
Growth-mindset individuals understand abilities can be developed through effort, teaching, and persistence.
Politics refers to the actions or activities concerned with achieving and using power in a nation or society.
Politics is also the ways that power is shared in an organization and affected by personal relationships.
Politics is concerned with power, and those who hold power hold influence.
Politics functions based on socio-economic and cultural context.
Political claims are influenced by geographical location and ideology.
The political is also personal; personal choices reflect personal politics.
Politics goes hand in hand with society, influencing beliefs, ethics, and behavior.
Active citizenship refers to structured engagement with political processes and everyday participation.
Active citizenship is sharing decisions affecting oneself and the community.
Participation is a fundamental right of citizenship and a practice of democracy.
Active citizenship instills change and influences society at large.
Active citizenship helps individuals embrace their individuality through choices and behavior.
Political self is shaped by personal and environmental factors.
Youth participation in politics can result in cultural and political paradigm shifts.
Filipino hospitality is reflected in warmly welcoming guests and tourists.
Filipinos show respect for elders through gestures like “Mano po” and using “po” and “opo”.
Filipinos maintain close family ties and prioritize family reunions.
Filipinos have a cheerful personality, often smiling even in adversity.
Self-sacrifice is evident in OFWs working abroad for the benefit of family.
Bayanihan is the spirit of communal unity and cooperation.
“Bahala Na” attitude means leaving everything in God’s hands, similar to “Hakuna Matata”.
Colonial mentality favors foreign products and beauty standards over local ones.
“Mañana” habit refers to procrastination.
“Ningas Kugon” refers to starting eagerly but quickly losing interest.
Pride may prevent Filipinos from apologizing and maintaining relationships.
Crab mentality involves resenting others’ achievements and pulling them down.
Filipino Time reflects habitual tardiness in commitments.
Filipino proverbs (Salawikain) convey lessons, values, and observations about life.
Superstitions are passed down and adapted according to Filipino experiences.
Myths and legends explain origins and teach valuable lessons.
Heroes and icons remind Filipinos of patriotism and nationalism.
Religion is a cultural system of shared beliefs and rituals providing ultimate meaning.
Religion is a form of culture, involves beliefs expressed in ritualized practices, and provides a sense of purpose.
Religion unites people into a moral community called a Church.
The spiritual self is the most intimate, inner, subjective part of the self.
Christianity is the world’s largest religion (31%), followed by Islam (23%), Hinduism (15%), Buddhism (7%), Chinese Traditionalism (4%), and Judaism (0.2%).
The Philippines is the only Christian country in Southeast Asia.
Filipinos express religiosity through baptism, fiestas, prayer, fellowship, communion, and other church activities.
Before Spanish colonization, Filipinos believed in animism and supernatural beings.
Supernatural beliefs are culturally specific and influence daily life events like marriage, job seeking, and moving homes.
Rituals serve functions such as ensuring good harvest, protection from ancestors, healing, luck, and conception.
Rituals have three fundamental characteristics: emotional awe, belief system expression, and symbolic reference.
Religion involves belief in, worship of, or obedience to supernatural powers controlling human destiny.
Religiousness is participation in rituals, beliefs, and community of an organized religion.
Spirituality is experiencing something beyond oneself and living to benefit others.
Spiritual identity addresses ultimate questions about life, purpose, and meaning, guiding behavior according to core values.
Magic is influencing events via supernatural forces, and practitioners can be magicians or sorcerers.
Sorcerers (mambabarang) practice malevolent witchcraft, targeting people (binarang).
Kulam refers to Filipino witchcraft or voodoo, with mangkukulam as the practitioner.
Albulario are Filipino folk healers or witch doctors.
Buddhism teaches life is full of suffering and can be transcended through meditation and wisdom.
Christianity teaches belief in the Trinitarian God and eternal life through faith in Jesus Christ.
Hinduism teaches the cycle of birth, death, rebirth (Karma), with sacred texts Vedas, Mahabharata, Ramayana.
Islam teaches submission to Allah, belief in one God, and the five pillars (Shahadah, Salat, Zakat, Hajj, Sawm).
Judaism teaches belief in God of Abraham, sacred Torah, and observance of festivals like Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, Pesach, Shavuot, and Sukkot.
Learning Competency: Determine the possible product/s or services that will meet the need.
In the previous lesson you learned how to identify the potential market and the market need.
In this self-learning module, you will be able to know the products or services that will meet the need of the consumers.
A product is a tangible item that is put on the market for acquisition, attention, or consumption.
Products are countable, touchable, and visible wherein a consumer can assess its durability by examining it.
Consumer products are those designed to satisfy the needs and want of the ultimate consumer.
Convenience goods are products that consumers want to buy with as little difficulty and physical effort as possible.
Shopping goods are consumer goods which customers compare based on suitability, quality, price, and style.
Specialty products have unique characteristics and buyers show strong brand loyalty.
Unsought products are consumer products that customers do not normally think of buying.
Industrial goods are those purchased by organizations for use in production or operations.
Capital goods are industrial products directly used in production.
Raw materials are industrial goods used in making other products.
Component parts are processed items assembled into a finished product.
Major equipment is used to make, process, or sell other goods.
Accessory equipment facilitates production but does not become part of the final product.
Operating supplies are convenience products of industrial markets purchased with minimal effort.
A service is an intangible item arising from the output of individuals.
The difference between products and services involves tangibility, production, storage, consumer participation, and ownership.
A customer need is a motive that prompts a customer to buy a product or service.
Customers need products to function properly to solve their problem or desire.
Customers have unique budgets when purchasing a product or service.
Convenience means products need to provide an easy solution.
Experience must be easy and not create more work for customers.
Design must be intuitive and simple to use.
Reliability means the product must function as advertised every time.
Performance means the product must help customers achieve their goals.
Efficiency saves customers time.
Compatibility means products should work with other items customers use.
Customers expect empathy when dealing with service providers.
Customers expect fairness in pricing and terms.
Customers expect transparency in business interactions.
Customers need control during the business interaction.
Customers need options when making a purchase.
Customers need information throughout their entire experience.
Customers need accessibility to service and support teams.
The first step to identify customers’ needs is through customer needs analysis.
Identifying customer needs ensures satisfaction and loyalty.
Assuming what a customer wants can drive them away.
Identifying needs allows cross-selling related products.
Customers are often unclear about what they need, so asking questions is important.
Satisfied customers are less likely to enter disputes or seek legal action.
To ensure customer satisfaction, you must correctly identify customers' needs.
To identify needs, you must listen and ask the right questions.
After identifying needs, always check for additional or related needs.
Use knowledge and experience to present the right products or services.
To meet customer needs, identify them through research or listening tools.
Distribute information to relevant stakeholders.
Create product features or content aligned with customer needs.
Literature reviews are a vital part of a research project or paper.
They are particularly important during graduate school.
This handout will focus on defining what a literature review is, how to organize and synthesize information, and what the different parts of a literature review are.
A literature review demonstrates your ability to research.
It also showcases your expertise on your chosen topic.
Including a literature review provides readers with prevalent theories and studies on your topic.
It also provides evaluations and comparisons of these studies and gaps in the literature.
This helps your reader understand your project or thesis better.
It also makes you a more credible and reliable author.
A literature review is not an annotated bibliography.
An annotated bibliography summarizes each article individually with little connection between them.
This is different from synthesizing information in a literature review.
A literature review is not an essay, report, or research paper.
A literature review does not answer a question or list summarized articles.
A literature review is a discussion of current published material on a topic.
It attempts to synthesize and evaluate the material according to the research question, thesis, or themes.
A literature review allows readers to know what is being said about the topic and how sources compare.
It also reveals gaps in the research.
Writing the Introduction and Literature Review
The purpose of the introduction is to familiarize the reader with your research problem.
The introduction should present relevant literature.
The introduction should define the scope of the research.
An introduction must contextualize the research by establishing key findings from relevant literature or real-world problems.
The introduction must present the research problem by identifying gaps or uncertainty in existing research.
The introduction must explain how the research addresses the problem.
The first component of an introduction is to establish key ideas and findings.
This component explains the importance and relevance of the topic.
This component reviews related literature to provide a foundation.
The second component is to portray the research problem.
This component highlights uncertainties or gaps in research.
This component argues for the need for further investigation.
The third component announces your approach.
This component explains how your research will address the problem.
This component provides objectives and outlines the scope.
A good introduction states the importance of the topic.
A good introduction reviews research findings or accepted knowledge.
A good introduction may define key terms or constructs.
A good introduction identifies gaps in existing research.
A good introduction outlines research objectives or questions.
A direct approach states the significance clearly.
An indirect approach shows high research activity in the field.
Research problems may be defined by arguing for a real-world issue.
Research problems may be defined by identifying research gaps.
Research problems may be defined by highlighting insufficiency in current studies.
Announcing your approach involves stating the objectives.
Announcing your approach involves posing research questions or hypotheses.
Announcing your approach involves summarizing your research design or method.
Announcing your approach includes indicating the scope and parameters of the study.
The literature review provides a comprehensive background of your research topic.
The literature review links your work to previous research and identifies gaps.
A literature review discusses theories and research related to your topic.
A literature review may provide historical development of the topic.
A literature review highlights where existing research is lacking.
A literature review summarizes methods and analyses used in related studies.
A literature review provides an overview of existing research.
A literature review identifies connections between studies.
A literature review critically assesses past research and identifies limitations.
A literature review explains how your research builds on or diverges from previous work.
Critical evaluation requires going beyond summarizing.
Critical evaluation requires fair and balanced discussion.
Literature reviewing should continue as the research progresses.
Writers should avoid sweeping generalizations without supporting evidence.
Writers should avoid cherry-picking favorable research.
Claims must be substantiated with citations or references to established facts.
Introductions use present tense for general truths.
Introductions use past tense for specific findings.
Writers should use cautious evaluative language.
An introduction and literature review must include key findings from relevant literature.
An introduction and literature review must include a clear research problem or gap.
An introduction and literature review must include research objectives.
An introduction and literature review must include research questions.
Effective introductions and literature reviews set up the context for research.
Effective introductions and literature reviews identify the research problem clearly.
Effective introductions and literature reviews present a comprehensive yet critical review of past work.
Effective introductions and literature reviews establish your contribution to the field.

Common questions include what computer engineers do, what skills are needed, and what qualities engineers possess.
Engineers should be curious, critical thinkers, creative, effective communicators, and collaborative.
Engineers should be adept at using computers, manage time well, be lifelong learners, have people skills, and be well-trained in their specialization.
Computer engineers design hardware to create specialized solutions or new technologies.
Computer engineers deploy technologies by installing and updating equipment.
Computer engineers test systems they create to ensure proper function and find inefficiencies.
Computer engineers design efficient hardware systems suited to specific tasks.
Civil engineers design and supervise construction projects such as roads, buildings, airports, tunnels, dams, and bridges.

To become an engineer, one must complete an accredited program, obtain professional registration, and pursue lifelong learning.
Engineering functions include research, design, development, testing, production, deployment, maintenance, management, sales, consulting, and teaching.
Engineers lead conceptual design, research, planning, innovation, development, and supervision.
Technologists assist in product development, supervision, technical sales, and hardware design.

This training is to inform all participants of the potential hazards of working with or near energized electrical equipment.
This is basic information, provided to keep employees safe.
The focus is on recognizing, avoiding and reporting electrical hazards.
This training is not intended to provide skills or knowledge to perform electrical tasks.
Participants will NOT be qualified to perform any electrical tasks at sites.
Participants will NOT be authorized to repair any electrical equipment.
Participants will be able to recognize real or potential electrical hazards.
Participants will be able to avoid personal injury and prevent others from being injured.
Participants will be able to report emergencies and unsafe electrical conditions.
Electricity is all around us.
We all use electrical devices every day.
Some may present safety hazards.
To be safe, we must properly handle all electrical devices.
Familiarity can sometimes contribute to taking risks.
Voltage is the force that causes electrical current to flow.
Current is the flow of electricity.
Resistance is opposition to current flow.
Conductors allow current to flow easily.
Insulators resist current flow.
A circuit is the path that current follows.
A short circuit is a low-resistance path that causes high current flow.
Current cannot flow unless there is a complete circuit.
Unintentional paths can be made by water, metal, dirt, chemicals, or a human body.
If the current path goes through you, you will receive a shock.
The human body is 50%â€“65% water.
The three hazards of electricity are shock, arc flash, and blast.
An electric shock is current flowing through the body.
Shocks can cause internal burns and organ damage.
Static shocks have high voltage but low current and are low risk.
Electrical circuit shocks have high current capability and are dangerous.
Effects of current levels on the body vary and can include pain, breathing stoppage, or heart failure.
Fuses and circuit breakers protect equipment but not people.
GFCIs protect people by tripping at 4â€“6 mA leakage.
Preventing electrical shocks requires using approved and well-maintained equipment and following safe practices.
If a hazard is found, stay away, keep others away, and report immediately.
In an electrical emergency, call for help and do not approach the victim.
Arc flash can cause severe burns and injuries.
Blast hazards are caused by rapid expansion during arc faults.
Hazards include damaged wires, broken covers, poor environmental conditions, and overloaded receptacles.
Good portable equipment practices include inspecting cords, avoiding defective receptacles, and pulling plugs correctly.

Naglabas si Pangulong Corazon Aquino ng EO 335 na nag-aatas sa paggamit ng Filipino sa opisyal na komunikasyon.
Ayon kina Lumbera et al., ang Filipino ay wika ng edukasyong nagtataguyod ng kapakanan ng bansa.
Inilathala ni David Michael San Juan noong Agosto 10, 2014 ang 12 Reasons to Save the National Language.
Ayon kay Virgilio Almario, marami pang dapat gawin para ganap na magtagumpay ang Filipino.
Ayon kay Manuel L. Quezon, walang pagkakaisa kung walang wikang panlahat.
Basketball is a type of indoor team sport played worldwide requiring teamwork, quick reactions, and endurance.
Dr. James Naismith introduced basketball on December 21, 1891.
Naismith was a Physical Education instructor at the School for Christian Workers in Massachusetts, USA.
Luther Gulick asked Naismith to create an indoor winter sport.
Naismith used a soccer ball and peach baskets for the first game.
The first basketball game occurred in December 1891.
Basketball is played by two teams of five players each.
The ball may be passed, thrown, tapped, rolled, or dribbled in any direction.
The winning team is the one with more points at the end of the fourth period.
The court measures 28 meters by 15 meters.
A basketball has a circumference of 29.5 inches and weighs 22 ounces.
A team may have up to 12 members in games with four periods of play.
A typical team consists of two guards, two forwards, and one center.
The point guard directs the attack and handles the ball.
The shooting guard scores points and steals the ball.
The center is usually the tallest player and a strong rebounder.
Dribbling, passing, and shooting are the basic elements of basketball.
Types of dribbling include control dribble, speed dribble, and crossover dribble.
Types of passing include bounce pass, chest pass, and overhead pass.
Types of shooting include jump shot, layup, hook shot, and dunking shot.
The game consists of four periods of ten minutes each.
If the score is tied, a five-minute extra period is played.
The game begins with a jump ball at center court.
A ball becomes live during a legal tap on a jump ball, a free throw, or a throw-in.
A ball becomes dead when a goal is made or when the whistle is blown.
An air ball is a shot that misses the rim or backboard.
A foul is a rule infraction involving illegal physical contact.
A violation is a rule infraction without physical contact.
A rebound occurs when a player retrieves the ball after a missed shot.
A time-out is a sixty-second stop in play for strategy and rest.
Basketball referees use standardized hand signals to communicate decisions during the game.
Stop the clock is signaled with an open palm.
Stop the clock for a foul is signaled with a clenched fist.
Start the clock is signaled with a chopping motion.
One point is signaled by raising one finger.
Two points are signaled using two fingers from the wrist.
Three points are signaled using three fingers, with one arm for attempt and two arms for a successful shot.
Substitution is signaled by crossing forearms.
A time-out is signaled by forming a T with the hands.
Media time-out is indicated by forming a T and showing the index finger.
Cancel score or cancel play is signaled with scissor-like arm actions.
A visible count is shown by moving the palm while counting.
Shot clock reset is signaled by rotating the hand.
Direction of play is indicated by pointing with the arm extended.
A jump ball direction is pointed with the arm parallel to the sidelines.
Travelling is signaled by rotating fists.
Double dribbling is signaled with a patting motion.
Carrying the ball is signaled by a half rotation of the palm.
A five-second violation is signaled by showing five fingers.
An eight-second violation is signaled by showing eight fingers.
A three-second violation is signaled by extending the arm and showing three fingers.
A 24-second violation is signaled by touching the shoulder.
A deliberate kick or block of the ball is signaled by pointing to the foot.
Ball returned to backcourt is signaled by waving the arm in front of the body.
Referees use specific hand signs to indicate player numbers.
Holding is signaled by grasping the wrist downward.
Pushing is signaled by imitating a push.
Blocking is signaled by placing both hands on the hips.
Hand checking is signaled by striking the open palm with a clenched fist.
Illegal contact to the hand is signaled by striking the palm toward the forearm.
Charging with the ball is signaled by swinging the elbow backward.
Foul on the act of shooting is signaled by raising an arm with a clenched fist and indicating numbers or free throws.
Foul not on the act of shooting is signaled with a counting motion.
Double foul is signaled by waving clenched fists on both hands.
Technical foul is signaled by forming a T with the hands.
Unsportsmanlike foul is signaled with clenched fists on both hands.
Housekeeping includes safety color standards used to identify hazards and emergency equipment.
Red indicates fire protection equipment.
Green indicates safety.
White, black, or both are used for traffic and housekeeping marking.
Yellow indicates caution for physical hazards.
Orange indicates dangerous machine parts and energized equipment.
Blue indicates precaution while equipment is under repair.
Purple indicates radiation hazards.
Employers must provide workers with safe drinking water, sanitary facilities, and proper sleeping quarters.
All workplaces, passageways, and storerooms must be kept clean and orderly.
Floors must be kept clean and dry, providing drainage where wet processes are used.
Workplaces must be kept free from protruding nails, splinters, holes, or loose boards.
5S housekeeping involves systematic organization and discipline in the workplace.
Companies like Hewlett-Packard use 5S to improve efficiency and productivity.
Sorting means keeping only important files and removing unnecessary ones.
Setting in order means organizing items for easy retrieval.
Shining means cleaning regularly to remove unnecessary data or clutter.
Standardizing ensures habits and processes are consistently followed.
Sustaining means monitoring adherence to all 5S practices.
Slips, trips, and falls are a major cause of workplace injuries.
Spills should be cleaned immediately to prevent accidents.
Aisles and exits must be kept clear to prevent accidents.
Worn or damaged flooring should be replaced to prevent falls.
Combustible materials should be stored properly to prevent fire hazards.
Flammable substances must be kept away from ignition sources.
Fire doors and passageways must remain unobstructed.
Materials must be stored at least 18 inches away from sprinkler systems.
Dust accumulation of 1/32 inch covering 5 percent of a surface can pose an explosion hazard.
Vacuuming is the preferred method for dust removal.
Falling objects can be prevented with toe boards, rails, or nets.
Heavy objects should be stored on lower shelves.
A cluttered workplace increases the risk of injury.
Materials no longer needed should be promptly stored or disposed of.
Storage areas should not contain materials that create tripping or fire hazards.
Ladders and tools must not block electrical panels.
Workers must use appropriate personal protective equipment for hazards.
Respirators protect workers from particles, chemicals, and airborne hazards.
Gloves provide protection from chemicals, pathogens, and contaminants.
Goggles protect workers from chemical splashes and harmful particles.
Slush boots protect workers in contaminated or hazardous areas.
All workers should participate in housekeeping by keeping their areas tidy and reporting hazards.
Housekeeping frequency depends on the amount of debris or contaminants produced.
Housekeeping policies should be written to define responsibilities and procedures.
Housekeeping should be maintained long-term through monitoring and auditing.
Bioethics is the study of ethical issues and dilemmas arising in healthcare, life sciences, and biotechnology.
It explores principles, theories, and frameworks to analyze ethical questions.
Bioethics develops critical thinking and ethical reasoning skills for moral challenges in research and healthcare.
Bioethics is a subfield of ethics focusing on ethical questions in life sciences.
It combines ethical theory with disciplines like medicine, law, and social sciences.
Medical ethics deals with moral values and decisions in medicine.
Bioethics is the ethics of biology subdivided into environmental, animal, and medical ethics.
It deals with relationships of humans with environment and nature.
Daniel Callahan first introduced the term bioethics in 1969.
Van Rensselaer Potter popularized the term in 1970.
Bioethics studies ethical issues from developments in life science technologies.
Nuremberg Code (1947) established principles for ethical human subject research.
Declaration of Helsinki (1964) provided research ethics guidelines.
Belmont Report (1979) introduced respect for persons, beneficence, and justice.
Roe v. Wade (1973) legalized abortion and sparked ethical debates.
Dolly the Sheep (1996) raised ethical concerns on cloning.
Human Genome Project raised issues on genetic privacy and discrimination.
CRISPR-Cas9 (2012) sparked debates on gene editing ethics.
Bioethics is needed because advances can bring both benefits and ethical risks.
Bioethics helps individuals and society address ethical decisions like steroid use, genetic testing, and vaccination policies.
Bioethical questions concern what people should do, while science concerns what is.
Ethics applies moral codes and guides professional behavior.
Four principles guide bioethics: autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, and justice.
Beginning of human life is debated and affects abortion ethics.
Neurological theory says life begins when EEG brain waves appear at 24–27 weeks.
Biological independence theory ties life to viability around 24 weeks.
Metabolic theory argues fertilization is a long process and timing is uncertain.
Embryological theory places the start of life at gastrulation.
Consciousness theory says life begins when self-awareness develops.
Genetic theory holds life begins at fertilization.
Sex selection can be done by sperm sorting, PGD, or post-implantation diagnosis.
IVF raises ethical issues about embryo selection and disposal.
Surrogacy raises concerns about exploitation and legal rights.
Palliative care balances pain relief and patient autonomy.
Physician-assisted suicide raises autonomy vs. sanctity-of-life debate.
Advance directives allow patients to express future medical decisions.
Organ transplantation replaces failing organs from living or deceased donors.
RA 7170 is the Organ Donation Act of 1992.
Stem cells can self-replicate and differentiate into specialized cells.
Cryonics freezes bodies with speculative hope of revival.
Jose Rizal’s Romances objectives are to know more about Rizal’s romances and appreciate his realizations in a deeper sense.
Another objective is to evaluate his ideals and principles when it comes to matters beyond his studies.
In 1877, Rizal’s first love was Segunda Katigbak.
Segunda was the sister of Rizal’s classmate Mariano Katigbak.
She studied at La Concordia College with Rizal’s sister Olympia.
They met either in Trozo, Manila or in Lipa, Batangas.
Rizal frequently visited La Concordia to see her.
Rizal saw Segunda pass by but chose to go home instead of following her.
Jacinta Ibarda Laza was a woman from Pakil, Laguna with attractive eyes.
Her relationship with Rizal ended because his memories of Segunda were still strong.
Rizal’s father objected because she was his professor.
In 1878, Rizal’s second love was Leonor Valenzuela.
From 1878–1890, his third love was Leonor Rivera.
Leonor Rivera was his childhood sweetheart and cousin.
They met while Rizal studied medicine at UST.
Their love lasted 8 to 11 years.
Their relationship ended due to unread letters, Noli Me Tangere, and Rivera’s engagement to Henry Kipping.
In 1884, Rizal met Consuelo Ortiga y Reyes.
In 1888, Rizal met O-Sei-San, a 23-year-old Japanese woman.
In 1886, he met Gertrude Beckett in London.
Gertrude helped Rizal complete several artworks.
Their relationship faded, and Rizal left for Paris.
In 1889, Rizal met Suzanne Jacoby in Brussels.
They spent time together during the city’s festival.
In 1891, Rizal met Nellie Boustead.
He considered marrying Nellie after Rivera married someone else.
The relationship ended because Nellie wanted him to convert to Protestantism.
In 1895, Rizal met Josephine Bracken.
Josephine visited Rizal in Dapitan with her adoptive father for treatment.
Rizal and Josephine fell in love and lived together.
Their son Francisco was stillborn.
After Rizal’s death, Josephine returned to Hong Kong and later married Vicente Abad.
Some accounts say she returned to the Philippines and taught English.
No priest agreed to marry Rizal and Josephine legally.
They performed a symbolic self-marriage before God.
Rizal said, “On this battlefield man has no better weapon than his intelligence, no other force but his heart.”
Marksmanship is the skillful art of shooting with a rifle and hitting the target at a given or known distance.
You must first be able to assume the correct firing positions, which will make you and your rifle a single steady unit.
Secondly, you must know how to correctly align your rifle with the target and fire the weapon without disturbing this alignment.
A soldier's development of good marksmanship skills is based on mastering proper aiming and keeping a proper and steady hold of the rifle.
Proper aiming requires the rear sight, the blades of the front sight, and the target to be aligned correctly.
This alignment is known as the proper sight picture.
The elements of a correct sight picture include sight alignment and placement of the aiming point.
Keeping a proper and steady hold allows the firer to hold the weapon steadily when aligning sights and firing.
Eight factors affect the proper holding of a rifle: left hand grip, rifle butt placement, right hand grip, right elbow placement, stock weld, breathing, relaxation, and trigger control.
A soldier must assume the steadiest firing position that provides observation and some cover in combat.
Firing positions include prone, sitting, kneeling, and standing.
Triangulation is a pre-marksmanship method that teaches proper sight alignment during dry firing.
The coach and pupil technique assigns a coach who guides, assists, and ensures range safety for the firer during training.
Abstract Data Type (ADT) is an abstraction of a data structure that provides only the interface to which the data structure must adhere.
An abstract data type (or ADT) defines some kind of data and the operations that can be performed on it.
It is a pure interface, with no mention of an implementation —that’s what makes it abstract.
Abstraction is a technique of hiding the internal details from the user and only showing the necessary details to the user.
Encapsulation is a technique of combining the data and the member function in a single unit.
List ADT stores data generally in key sequence in a list with a head structure consisting of count, pointers, and address of a compare function.
Each data node in List ADT contains a pointer to a data structure and a self-referential pointer which points to the next node in the list.
List ADT supports operations like get(), insert(), remove(), removeAt(), replace(), size(), isEmpty(), and isFull().
Stack ADT stores a pointer to the data, allocates memory for the data, and passes the address to the stack.
Stack ADT supports push(), pop(), peek(), size(), isEmpty(), and isFull() operations.
Queue ADT follows the design of Stack ADT; each node contains a void pointer to the data and a link pointer to the next element in the queue.
Queue ADT supports enqueue(), dequeue(), peek(), size(), isEmpty(), and isFull() operations.
Features of ADT include abstraction, encapsulation, data structure independence, information hiding, and modularity.
Advantages of ADT include better conceptualization, robust encapsulation, data abstraction, and modularity.
Disadvantages of ADT include overhead, complexity, learning curve, limited flexibility, and cost.
Engineers should understand the traits, uses, and polymeric material processing because polymers are widely used in applications including microelectronics and building materials.
Recognizing the processes via which the elastic and plastic deformation of polymers occurs enables modification and control of strength and elasticity.
The stress–strain behavior links external forces applied to a polymer with the resulting deformation, which is crucial for predicting performance and reliability.
Elastomers are polymers with long carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, or silicon chains, highly elastic and viscous, with intermolecular cross-links.
Viscoelastic materials exhibit both viscous and elastic properties, showing time-dependent deformation under stress.
Fracture in polymers occurs when stress exceeds tensile strength, with thermosetting polymers generally exhibiting brittle fracture and thermoplastics showing ductile-to-brittle transitions.
Factors such as temperature, strain rate, specimen thickness, and polymer structure influence fracture behavior.
Mechanical properties of semicrystalline polymers are influenced by molecular weight, predeformation (drawing), and heat-treating (annealing).
Elastic deformation is reversible, whereas plastic deformation is permanent and can exhibit strain hardening.
Crystallization involves alignment of polymer chains into ordered chain-folded crystallites.
Melting corresponds to the transformation of crystalline polymer regions from solid ordered chains to a viscous, disordered liquid.
The glass transition is a gradual transformation of amorphous or semicrystalline polymers from liquid to rubbery and finally rigid solid as temperature decreases.
Plastics are polymers with structural stiffness under load, used in general-purpose applications, including polyesters, polyethylene, polypropylene, poly(vinyl chloride), and polystyrene.
Fibers can be natural (cotton, wool, silk) or synthetic (polyester, nylon), composed of long polymer chains.
Polymer additives such as fillers, plasticizers, stabilizers, colorants, and flame retardants modify properties like strength, flexibility, durability, and resistance.
Common forming techniques for plastics include molding (compression, transfer, injection, blow, extrusion) and casting.
Fabrication of elastomers and fibers often uses similar processes, including compression molding, extrusion, spinning, and film extrusion.
Software Testing is the process of assessing the functionality of a software program.
It involves identifying and verifying that software applications or programs will meet the user’s requirements.

The Software Testing Life Cycle (STLC) includes phases such as test planning, test case development, test environment setup, test execution, defect management and retesting, regression testing, test reporting, and test closure.
Test planning defines objectives, scope, strategy, and allocation of resources for testing.
Test cases are created based on requirements and design documents, covering both positive and negative scenarios.
Test environment setup involves configuring hardware, software, and network infrastructure necessary for testing.
Test execution involves running test cases and comparing results with expected outcomes to identify defects.
Defect management and retesting ensure defects are reported, tracked, and verified after fixes.
Regression testing verifies that changes or fixes do not introduce new defects or break existing functionality.
Test reporting summarizes activities, coverage, defects found, and resolution status.
Test closure evaluates the testing process, archives test assets, documents lessons learned, and gathers feedback.
Software Testing Levels include Unit Testing, Integration Testing, System Testing, and Acceptance Testing.
Unit Testing verifies individual modules or new/refactored code against requirements.
Integration Testing evaluates whether combined units work correctly together.
System Testing tests the complete software system for functional and non-functional requirements in an environment similar to production.
Acceptance Testing (User Acceptance Testing or UAT) validates the product against end-user requirements before final acceptance.
Testing Methods include Manual Testing, Automated Testing, Continuous Testing, Black-Box Testing, White-Box Testing, Grey-Box Testing, and Security-Focused Testing.
Manual Testing requires human testers to execute test cases and validate software functionality.
Automated Testing uses tools to run tests and reduce human effort.
Continuous Testing integrates testing into the software delivery pipeline to provide rapid feedback and early issue detection.
Black-Box Testing examines software functionality without knowledge of internal structures.
White-Box Testing inspects internal logic and structure of the code.
Grey-Box Testing combines limited knowledge of internal workings with functional testing.
Security-Focused Testing protects software from evolving digital threats.

Software Testing Best Practices include involving users, planning for scalability and adaptability, writing tests before coding, thorough reporting, dividing tests into smaller units, two-tier test automation, and not skipping regression testing.
Debugging is the process of identifying and fixing errors, bugs, or unexpected behavior in software.
Effective debugging is a crucial skill for developers, involving systematic problem-solving and understanding of code.
In Python, the try block lets you test a block of code for errors.
The except block lets you handle the error.
The else block executes code when there is no error.
The finally block executes code regardless of the result of the try and except blocks.
Print statement debugging involves inserting print statements in the code to output variable values or checkpoints.
Code inspection involves reviewing the code for syntax errors, logical issues, or common mistakes.
Using a debugger allows stepping through code, inspecting variables, and identifying issues.
Isolation of code involves temporarily removing or isolating sections to identify the source of a problem.
Peer review involves seeking assistance from colleagues to review the code and provide insights.
Version control examination involves checking version history to identify recent changes related to bugs.
File handling in Python allows performing a wide range of operations such as data storage, sharing, and configuration management.
Python handles text files (.txt, .csv) and binary files (images, audio, video, .bin, .dat).
File operations in Python include opening, reading, writing, appending, and closing files.
The open() function is used to open files with modes like 'r', 'w', 'a', 'rb', etc.
Reading from a file uses read(), readline(), or readlines() methods.
Writing to a file uses write() or writelines() methods.
Binary files are handled with 'rb' and 'wb' modes.
File handling best practices include closing files, handling exceptions, validating paths, and avoiding unintentional overwrites.
Real-world applications of file handling include logging, monitoring, data processing (CSV, JSON), and configuration management.

The single biggest threat to man’s continued dominance on the planet is the virus.
According to Merriam-Webster, a virus is a submicroscopic infectious agent.
It is usually regarded as non-living molecules with RNA or DNA covered in a protein coat.
A virus is not visible using a light microscope.
It can reproduce inside a host and causes various diseases in humans, animals, and plants.
According to Cleaveland Clinic, a virus is a microscopic organism that can infect hosts such as humans, plants, and animals.
It is a small piece of genetic information (DNA or RNA) inside a protective casing called a capsid.
A virus can’t reproduce without a host.
According to LibreTexts, a virus is visible using an electron microscope.
Viruses are not included in the Tree of Life because they are acellular.
Viruses are obligate intracellular parasites because they need the host to live and reproduce.
Viruses enter their host and produce viral components, which are proteins and nucleic acids needed to form new virus particles called virions.
Living things have cells, but viruses do not have cells.
Viruses have a protein coat that protects their genetic material but do not have a cell membrane or other organelles.
Living things reproduce by making a copy of their DNA, but viruses lack the tools to do this.
Outside of a host cell, viruses do not use energy; they become active only when in contact with a host cell.
The Baltimore classification system categorizes viruses based on nucleic acid type and replication strategy.
It distinguishes single-stranded RNA viruses into positive-sense (+) and negative-sense (−) strands.
Positive-sense RNA can be directly translated into proteins, while negative-sense RNA must first be transcribed into positive-sense RNA.
The system also accounts for viruses capable of reverse transcription, converting RNA into DNA.
Virus morphology: they are small intracellular parasites with a complete virus particle called a virion containing DNA or RNA genome.
The viral genome is packaged inside a protein capsid, and nucleocapsid proteins form the nucleocapsid.
Enveloped viruses have a lipid bilayer derived from host cell membrane and studded with viral glycoproteins.
Viruses may be helical, with capsid proteins wrapped around nucleic acid, forming an elongated tube.
Viruses may be icosahedral, with spherical shape and 20 triangular faces.
Some viruses are complex or irregular, like poxvirus or bacteriophage.
According to News Medical & Life Sciences, viruses cannot reproduce on their own; they depend on host cells.
Common examples of viruses include HIV, Influenza A virus, Hepatitis B virus, SARS-CoV-2, and Ebola virus.
A function is a module that returns a value back to the part of the program that called it.
Many languages provide libraries of functions that you can use, such as Random Number Generator.
A function is like a module, but it returns a value that can be used in your program.
Library functions are written functions that come with most languages.
They perform common tasks and save time for the programmer by allowing code reuse.
The Random Number Generator function is useful in game programs, simulation programs, statistical programs, and computer security such as encryption.
A random number function works by using arguments that define the range, e.g., random(1,100), and returns a value assigned to a variable.
Most languages allow coders to write functions.
The function header specifies the data type of the value returned, the name of the function, and any parameter variables.
The function body contains the statements that execute when the function is called.
The return statement specifies the value returned when the function ends.
Functions simplify code, increase development speed, and facilitate teamwork.
Each function should be flowcharted separately.
IPO (input, processing, output) can be used to show what a function does.
Mathematical functions typically accept one or more values as arguments, perform a mathematical operation, and return results.
math.sqrt(16) returns the square root of 16.
pow(4,2) raises the value of 4 to the power of 2.
math.fabs() calculates the absolute value of a number.
math.cos() returns the cosine of an argument.
math.sin() returns the sine of an argument.
math.tan() returns the tangent of an argument.
round() rounds a number to the nearest integer.
Data type conversion functions convert values from one data type to another.
int() converts a real to an integer.
float() converts an integer to a real/float.
Real numbers can store integers, but integers cannot store real numbers.
Type mismatch errors will occur without converting values.
String functions allow for working with strings.
length() returns the length of a string.
append() joins multiple strings together.
toUpper() and toLower() convert a string to upper or lower case.
substring() extracts a character or a portion of a string.
contains() identifies similar strings within two strings.
string to integer and string to float functions convert strings storing numbers into number data types.
type == str / float / int can test numbers to see if they can be converted to a string.
